Withstand fluctuations in deposit levels by performing periodic reviews of the Bank’s deposit structure;



Provide for customer credit needs;



Maximize net interest income within acceptable levels of liquidity and interest rate risk within capital adequacy constraints;



Manage assets and liabilities so the Bank does not find itself in the position of having to liquidate assets or purchase liabilities at unfavorable cost levels;



Provide a contingency plan that addresses potential adverse liquidity crisis events, emergency cash flow requirements, and alternative sources of funds if initial projections of funding sources and uses are incorrect, including periodic tests of their use;



Ensure approvals are properly obtained and documented for exceptions to policies, limits and authorizations;



Maintain adequate tax planning;



Provide authority and oversight to access wholesale funding sources, including the types and terms of each permitted wholesale funding source and the process for measuring and monitoring unused borrowing capacity;



Coordinate the Bank’s liquidity risk management with disaster, contingency, strategic planning, and budgeting efforts, in addition to business line and risk management objectives, strategies, and tactics; and



Provide an adequate system of internal controls that ensures the independent and periodic review of the Bank’s liquidity management process, and compliance with this and other related policies and procedures.



Other written policies with regard to selected portions of the Bank's resource mix, such as the INVESTMENT POLICY, INTEREST RATE RISK POLICY, LOAN LOSS RESERVE (ALLL) POLICY, GENERAL LOAN POLICY, COMMERCIAL LOAN POLICY, CONSUMER LOAN POLICY, REAL ESTATE LOAN POLICY, and any specific liability management policies are to coordinate with or act as subsets of this master policy.



Enforcement



Changes to this policy require approval by the Board of Directors of the Bank.  Changes in operating procedures, standards, guidelines and technologies, provided they are consistent with this policy, may be authorized by the OFFICER.



The Board of Directors has the authority to approve this policy, and annually approves the merit thereafter.  Senior Management is responsible for ensuring the directives are implemented and administered in compliance with the approved policy.



The primary responsibility for enforcement of this policy and its operating procedures rests with the OFFICER and our employees.



No part of this policy or its supporting operating procedures should be interpreted as contravening or superseding any other legal and regulatory requirements placed upon the Bank.  Protective measures should not impede other legally mandated processes such as records retention or subpoenas.  Any conflicts should be submitted immediately to the DEPARTMENT for further evaluation and/or subsequent submission to the Bank’s legal counsel.



Exceptions to Policy



Requests for exceptions to this policy must be very specific and may only be granted on specific items, rather than to entire sections.  Bank personnel with exceptions are to communicate their requests by submitting an internal memorandum to the OFFICER for consideration by Senior Management.



3.	DEFINITIONS



Liquidity.  In general, liquidity is:



The ability to fund assets and meet obligations as they come due;



The amount the Bank holds in cash and other assets that are quickly convertible into cash without significant loss;



The Bank’s capacity to meet its financial obligations and commitments at reasonable or acceptable costs.



The essence of having cash when the Bank needs it.



Maintaining an adequate level of liquidity depends on the Bank’s ability to efficiently meet both expected and unexpected cash flows and collateral needs without adversely affecting either daily operations or the financial condition of the Bank.



Liquid Assets.  Liquid assets include any excess cash the Bank may have either in its vaults or on deposit with a Federal Reserve Bank or a correspondent Bank.  It also includes any non-loan earning asset that is unpledged and less than TIME PERIOD from maturity.  Types of liquid assets are listed below:



Cash on hand (including items in the process of collection);



Demand deposits due from banks;



U.S. treasury bills and notes;



Obligations due from federal agencies;



Certificates of deposit with other banks and savings and loan institutions due within TIME PERIOD;



Federal funds sold;



Money market instruments;



Municipal bonds due within TIME PERIOD.



Liquidity Risk.  The risk that the Bank’s financial condition or overall safety and soundness is adversely affected by an inability (or perceived inability) to meet its obligations.  The Bank’s obligations, and the funding sources used to meet them, depend significantly on its business mix, balance sheet structure, and the cash flow profiles of its on and off-balance sheet obligations.  In managing its cash flows, the Bank may confront various situations that can give rise to increased liquidity risk.  These include funding mismatches, market constraints on the ability to convert assets into cash or in accessing sources of funds (i.e., market liquidity), and contingent liquidity events.  Changes in economic conditions or exposure to credit, market, operation, legal, and reputation risks also can affect the Bank’s liquidity risk profile and should be considered in the assessment of liquidity and asset/liability management.



Non-Liquid Assets.  All other assets are deemed to be non-liquid.



Volatile Liabilities.  Volatile liabilities are deposits the Bank cannot depend on remaining with the Bank and include any liabilities the Bank may be forced to pay within the next six months.



Reliable Liabilities.  All other liabilities are deemed to be reliable.



4.	RISK MANAGEMENT



General



The Bank has identified the following primary risks that may affect its liquidity:



Reputation Risk.  Reputation risk is the current and prospective impact on earnings and capital arising from negative public opinion.  The Bank's reputation for meeting its obligations and operating in a safe and sound manner is essential to attracting funds at a reasonable cost and retaining funds during troubled times.  Negative public opinion, whatever the cause, may prompt depositors, other funds providers, and investors to seek greater compensation, such as higher rates or additional credit support, for maintaining deposit balances with the Bank or conducting any other business with it.  If negative public opinion continues, withdrawals of funding could become debilitating.  To minimize reputation risk and its potential impact on liquidity, the Bank:



Continually assesses its reliance on credit sensitive funding;



Diversifies the sources and tenors of market funding; and



Increases asset liquidity, as appropriate.



Strategic Risk.  Strategic risk is the current and prospective impact on earnings or capital arising from adverse business decisions, improper implementation of decisions, or lack of responsiveness to industry changes.  As such, the Bank periodically considers the impact on its funding abilities when creating strategic goals, objectives or during times of rapid growth to avoid liquidity risk to reach an unacceptable level.  In this respect, the Bank must be able to raise money required to meet its obligations at an affordable cost.



Credit Risk.  Credit risk is the current and prospective risk to earnings or capital arising from an obligor’s failure to meet the terms of any contract with the Bank or otherwise to perform as agreed.  In the event the Bank decides to assume more credit risk through asset concentrations or adoption of new underwriting standards in conjunction with untested business lines, it will properly evaluate the impact this action will have on its liquidity risk.



Interest Rate Risk.  Interest rate risk is the current and prospective risk to earnings or capital arising from movements in interest rates.  Changes in interest rates affect income earned from assets and the cost of funding those assets.  A change in interest rates also affects the economic value of the Bank’s balance sheet.  For example, the present value of most investment securities decreases in a rising rate environment.  To maintain the total value of assets serving as collateral in repurchase agreements or pledged against deposits, the Bank may have to pledge or encumber additional securities, increasing its cost of funds.  The cost of alternative funding sources may also increase as depositors and other lenders demand market interest rates in a rising rate environment.  Off-balance sheet instruments that the Bank uses to manage its interest rate risk may also pose liquidity risk.  The cash flows of those instruments are often very sensitive to changes in rates, and if not properly managed, can result in unexpected funding requirements or other cash outflows during periods of volatile interest rates.



Refer to the Bank’s INTEREST RATE RISK POLICY for detailed guidance.



Price Risk.  Price risk (or market risk) is the risk to earnings or capital arising from changes in the value of traded portfolios of financial instruments.  Price risk may result in volatile earnings.  Price risk is closely monitored by funds providers when assessing the Bank's financial position and creditworthiness.  If price risk and its perceived impact on earnings or capital is too great, funds providers may require the Bank to pay increased rates for funds, may not be willing to invest in longer term maturities, or may not be willing to provide funding on any terms.



Transaction Risk.  Transaction risk is the current and prospective risk to earnings and capital arising from fraud, error, and the inability to deliver products or services, maintain a competitive position and manage information.  Systems that directly affect liquidity include wire transfer systems for check and securities clearing, electronic banking, and operations governing credit, debit, and smart card usage.  If product lines change, the Bank must adjust the systems to ensure that all transactions can be handled.  Significant problems can develop very quickly if the systems that process transactions fail or delay execution.  If customers have difficulty accessing their accounts, they may close them, which will diminish liquidity.  As such, transaction risk is considered in the Bank's contingency planning process.



Liability Based or Off-balance Sheet Strategy Risk



With respect to the use of liability based or off-balance sheet liquidity strategies, the Bank realizes that traditional measures of liquidity, such as the ratio of loans to deposits or non-core funding dependency, may not provide an accurate view of the Bank's true liquidity position.  In addition, an increase in use of liability based and off-balance sheet strategies will elevate the Bank’s liquidity risk profile.  As such, it is the policy of the Bank to take a forward looking approach to this liquidity planning strategy.  The Bank augments traditional liquidity risk measures with pro forma cash flow and scenario analysis to ensure that it maintains realistic contingency funding plans that are responsive to changes in liquidity risk exposure.  In this respect, the Bank utilizes liquidity measurement tools that match its funds management strategies that provide a comprehensive view of the Bank's liquidity risk.  Risk limits are approved by the Board of Directors and are to be consistent with the measurement tools used.



The Bank realizes that obtaining or retaining funding sources during times of financial stress may be difficult.  In this respect, the terms associated with wholesale borrowings (both secured and unsecured) can become more restrictive when the Bank faces either real or perceived financial difficulties.  In the event the Bank becomes less than “well capitalized” in accordance with Section 38 of the Federal Deposit Insurance Act, it may face limits on the ability to accept, renew, or roll over brokered or high cost deposits in accordance with the Joint Agency Advisory on Brokered and Rate Sensitive Deposits.  In addition, if the Bank uses securitization it can face early amortization events resulting from market disturbances, deterioration in the Bank's financial condition, or the performance of securitized assets.  These risks are more pronounced if the Bank has concentrated business lines or funding sources.



The Bank utilizes pro forma cash flow statements as part of its liquidity analysis for liability based or off-balance sheet funding strategies that hold significant amounts of assets with cash flows that depend on choices borrowers can make to prepay or extend their obligations, or that have off-balance sheet funding commitments.  Pro forma cash flow analysis shows the Bank's projected sources and uses of funds under various liquidity scenarios, identifying potential funding shortfalls or gaps.  It is the responsibility of the Chief Financial Officer and Senior Management to limit these exposures and report them to the Board of Directors along with a plan to address significant potential funding shortfalls which is commensurate with the complexity of the Bank’s liquidity risk profile.  The pro forma cash flow analysis is to incorporate multiple scenarios that consider the general and unique risks faced by the Bank.  In addition, assumptions used in the pro forma cash flow projections are to be reasonable and appropriate, and are to consider a wide range of potential outcomes with regard to the stability of retail and larger deposits, brokered deposits, public funds, borrowings, Internet deposits, and the retention rate of funds obtained through deposit specials.  In the event the Bank relies on securitization, sensitivity tests are to be performed to measure the effects that material changes to assumptions would have on related accounts.



Risk Mitigation



It is the responsibility of ALCO and Senior Management to monitor various internal and external market indicators of potential liquidity problems at the Bank.  These indicators, while not necessarily requiring drastic corrective action, may prompt ALCO, Senior Management and the Board of Directors to conduct additional monitoring or analysis procedures.  Examples of such internal indicators are:



A negative trend or significantly increased risk in any area or product line, such as loan delinquencies, cancellation of loan commitments, and/or not renewing maturing loans.



Concentrations in either assets or liabilities;



A decline in indicators of asset quality;



A decline in earnings performance or projections;



Rapid asset growth funded by volatile wholesale liabilities or brokered deposits;



Real or perceived negative publicity, such as negative third party opinions and evaluations from professional market analysts or other participants regarding concerns about the Bank’s credit capacity, including but not limited to:



The description of a “troubled bank” in market rumors;



Downgrades or announcements of potential downgrades of the Bank’s credit rating by agencies; or



Customers contacting relationship managers, fixed income sales representatives, and branch employees requesting information.



Bearish secondary market activity in the Bank's securities may signal declining value, such as:



Drop in stock price;



Wider secondary spreads on the Bank's senior and subordinated debt, and increasing trading of the Bank's debt; or



Brokers/dealers are reluctant to show the Bank's name in the market, forcing Senior Management to arrange "friendly" broker/dealer support.



The Bank's funding market may begin to contract or demand credit support, better credit terms, or shorter duration lending, any of which may increase liquidity costs, such as:



Overall funding costs increase;



Counterparties increase collateral requirements or demand collateral for accepting credit exposure to the Bank;



Correspondent banks eliminate or decrease credit line availability, causing the Bank to make larger purchases in the brokered funds market;



Volume of turndowns in the brokered markets is unusually large, forcing the Bank to deal directly with fewer willing counterparties;



Rating sensitive providers, such as trust managers, money managers, and public entities, abandon the Bank;



Counterparties and brokers are unwilling to deal in unsecured or longer term transactions;



Transaction sizes are decreasing, and some counterparties are unwilling to enter into even short dated transactions;



The Bank receives requests from depositors for early withdrawal of their funds, or the Bank has to repurchase its paper in the market; or



An increasing spread paid on deposits relative to local competitors, or national or regional composites.



Significant acquisitions by the Bank;



Decreasing weighted average maturity of liabilities;



Violation of self imposed liquidity limits;



Over reliance on or significant increase in the level of wholesale funding;



Excessive borrowing concentrations;



Significant concentrations within the asset portfolio or of funding sources;



Increasing deposit withdrawals;



A sharp rise in funding costs;



A sharp decline in earnings and interest margins;



Increase in spread paid on deposits, borrowed funds, or asset securitizations between the Bank and its peers;



Short interest or position (when a person sells stocks they do not yet own) in the Bank (or its holding company), thus leading investors to believe the Bank’s stock price will drop;



Pressure on the Bank to buy back loans or securities the Bank sold in the secondary market;



A strategy of granting higher risk loan products such as subprime loans and nontraditional loans, (option ARM loans, interest only loans, etc.) with a heavy reliance on selling into the secondary markets, particularly when the Bank funds these originations from wholesale funding sources;



An increase in nonperforming assets;



A decline in capital adequacy category;



Management problems; or



A sharp increase in putbacks of loans previously sold.



Liquidity Risk Limit Guidelines



The Bank has established liquidity risk tolerances and/or limits that are appropriate for its business strategy, taking into consideration the Bank’s complexity, business mix, liquidity risk profile, and its role in the financial system, including quantitative targets and qualitative guidelines.  These limits, tolerances, and guidelines may include items such as:



Discrete or cumulative cash flow mismatches or gaps (sources and uses of funds) over specified future short and long term time horizons under both expected and adverse business conditions.  Often, these are expressed as cash flow coverage ratios or as specific aggregate amounts.



Target amounts of unencumbered liquid asset reserves expressed as aggregate amounts or as ratios.



Measures used to identify unstable liabilities and liquid asset coverage ratios.  For example, these may include ratios of wholesale funding to total liabilities, potentially volatile retail (e.g., high cost or out of market) deposits to total deposits, and other liability dependency measures, such as short term borrowings as a percent of total funding. 



Asset concentrations that could increase liquidity risk through a limited ability to convert to cash, such as complex financial instruments (which are illiquid, difficult to value, or marked by the presence of cash flows that are irregular, uncertain or difficult to model), bank owned (corporate owned life insurance, and less marketable loan portfolios).



Funding concentrations that address diversification of funding sources and types, such as large liability and borrowed funds dependency, secured versus unsecured funding sources, exposures to single providers of funds, exposures to funds providers by market segments, and different types of brokered deposits or wholesale funding.



Funding concentrations that address the term, repricing, and market characteristics of funding sources with consideration given to the nature of the assets they fund.  This may include diversification targets for short, medium, and long term funding, instrument type and securitization vehicles, and guidance on concentrations for currencies and geographical markets.



Contingent liability exposures such as unfunded loan commitments, lines of credit supporting asset sales or securitizations, and collateral requirements for derivatives transactions and various types of secured lending.



Exposures of material activities, such as securitization, derivatives, trading, transaction processing, and international activities, to broad systemic and adverse financial market events.



Alternative measures and conditions which may be appropriate.



In addition, the Bank utilizes other risk indicators to specify risk tolerances.  These may include, but are not limited to the maximum ratio of:



Loans to deposits – LIST RATIO;



Loans to equity capital – LIST RATIO ;



Projected cash flow shortfall tolerated for a specified time (for example, one week ahead, one month ahead, one quarter ahead) as a percentage of liquid assets and unused borrowing facilities – LIST RATIOS;



Purchased funds to total assets – LIST RATIO;



Liquid assets to total assets – LIST RATIO;



Overnight borrowings to total assets – LIST RATIO;



FHLB advances to total assets – LIST RATIO;



Brokered deposits to total assets – LIST RATIO;



Total wholesale borrowings to total assets – LIST RATIO;



Pledged assets to total assets – LIST RATIO;



Managed assets to total assets in the event the Bank securitizes assets – LIST RATIO; and



Other common measures – DESCRIBE HERE AND LIST RATIOS.



However, in developing and using such measures, it is the responsibility of the Chief Financial Officer, ALCO, Senior Management, and the Board of Directors to be fully aware that some measures may not appropriately assess the time dimension and specific characteristics of the Bank’s liquidity risk profile.  Static balance sheet measures may not reveal significant liquidity risk that may exist under either normal or adverse business conditions and generally should not be the sole measure used to monitor and manage liquidity.



The scope and frequency of the Bank's internal liquidity risk management reports are based upon the complexity of the Bank's operations and risk profile.  These reports are designed to provide aggregate information with sufficient supporting detail to enable management to assess the sensitivity of the Bank to changes in market conditions, its own financial performance, and other important risk factors.  Reportable items may include, but are not limited to:



Cash flow gaps;



Cash flow projections (and the critical assumptions used);



Asset and funding concentrations;



Key early warning or risk indicators;



Funding availability;



Status of contingent funding sources;



Collateral usage; and



Use of and availability of government support (such as lending and guarantee programs) and implications on liquidity positions, particularly since these programs are generally temporary or reserved as a source for contingent funding.



Internal Controls



It is the policy of the Bank to maintain adequate internal controls to ensure the integrity of the Bank’s liquidity risk management process.  The Bank’s system of internal controls is designed to promote effective operations, reliable financial and regulatory reporting, and compliance with relevant laws and internal policies and procedures.  Internal control systems employed by the Bank are intended to provide appropriate approval processes, limits, and ensure regular and independent evaluation and review of the liquidity risk management process.  Such reviews address any significant changes in the nature of the instruments acquired, limits, and controls since the last review.  In general, the Bank’s internal controls are designed to provide:



Approval of exceptions to policies, limits and authorizations.  Positions that exceed established limits are to receive prompt attention from ALCO, Senior Management and the Board of Directors to ensure that the issue is resolved in accordance with this and other related policies, procedures and processes.



A schedule for the periodic review of this and the Bank’s other related policies, procedures and processes.  Periodic reviews of the liquidity management process and related procedures are to address any significant changes in liquidity risk limits, liquidity strategy, information systems, and internal controls since the last review.  Please refer to the Audit Policy topic of the policy for detailed guidance.



Contingency planning.  It is the responsibility of ALCO, Senior Management and the Board of Directors to properly assess the Bank’s anticipated responses to liquidity events in the context of their implications for the Bank’s short, intermediate, and long term liquidity profile.  Please refer to the Contingency Funding Plan topic of this policy for detailed guidance.



5.	RESPONSIBILITY



General



It is the responsibility of the Board of Directors and Senior Management to ensure that capital and liquidity levels are adequate and that appropriate capital and liquidity planning processes are in place.  This includes ensuring compliance with the conditions set forth in the initial approval letter or subsequent licensing decisions in addition to considering ongoing capital and liquidity needs.  As such, it is the policy of the Bank to implement Board of Directors’ approved capital and liquidity policies that outline the Board of Directors’ philosophy and articulate responsibilities and expectations for Senior Management of capital and liquidity for the Bank.  This and other related policies or procedures also define acceptable levels of capital and liquidity, establish a regular monitoring program, and include contingency funding plans that identify alternatives for meeting unanticipated needs.



In summary, the Board of Directors is responsible for:



Understanding the nature and level of the Bank’s liquidity risk profile and periodically reviewing information necessary to maintain this understanding;



Establishing and guiding the Bank's strategic direction and tolerance for liquidity risk, ensuring that it is communicated in such a manner that all levels of management clearly understand the Bank’s approach to managing the tradeoff’s between liquidity risk and short term profits;



Formally approving this and other significant strategies, policies, procedures and processes related to liquidity risk management on at least an annual basis;



Selecting members of Senior Management who will have the authority and responsibility to manage liquidity risk;



Monitoring the Bank's performance and overall liquidity risk profile;



Ensuring that liquidity risk is identified, measured, monitored and controlled by:



Establishing procedures, guidelines, internal controls and limits for managing and monitoring liquidity to ensure adequate liquidity is maintained at all times;



Understanding, preparing, and periodically reviewing contingency funding plans for handling potential adverse liquidity events;



Reviewing the Bank’s liquidity position on a regular basis and monitoring internal and external factors and events that could have a bearing on the Bank's liquidity; and



Periodically reviewing the Bank's liquidity strategies, policies and procedures.



Understanding the liquidity risk profiles of important subsidiaries and affiliates as appropriate.



In addition, the Board of Directors is responsible for regularly assessing Senior Management’s adherence to the Bank’s established policies and procedures, and evaluating capital adequacy and overall levels and trends in liquidity.  In general terms, this oversight process is to assess the appropriateness of the Bank’s level of:



Liquidity on at least a quarterly basis; and



Capital on at least an annual basis.



A more frequent analysis of the Bank’s capital and/or liquidity is warranted if the Bank:



Has not achieved stable profitability;



Is experiencing rapid growth, offering new products, or entering into new business lines;



Offers fiduciary services that are concentrated by revenue source or account officer; or



Has other conditions that increase the Bank’s risk profile.



Senior Management



Senior Management is responsible for:



Overseeing the daily and long term management of the Bank’s liquidity risk, position, and monitoring of internal and external factors and events that could have a bearing on the Bank’s liquidity;



Developing, implementing and periodically reviewing the Bank’s liquidity strategies, policies, procedures and practices that translate the Board of Directors’ goals, objectives, intent and risk tolerances into operating standards that are well understood by Bank personnel;



Performing sound forecasting and analysis, thoughtful contingency planning, and diversification and management of funding sources;



Adhering to the lines of authority and responsibility that the Board of Directors has established for managing liquidity risk;



Overseeing the development, implementation and maintenance of:



Appropriate risk measurement, management information, and other reporting systems that identify, measure, monitor and control the Bank's liquidity risk;



Liquid buffers (e.g., cash, unencumbered marketable securities, and market instruments); and



Contingency funding plans.



Fully understanding the Bank’s borrowing programs and rating system under the FHLB and Federal Reserve;



Periodically reviewing borrowing agreements and fully understanding all embedded triggers and parameters;



Establishing effective internal controls over the liquidity risk management process;



Monitoring and controlling liquidity risk exposures and funding needs within and across currencies, legal entities and subsidiaries, and business lines (and the group as a whole) on an ongoing basis, and ensuring that the group’s senior management is actively monitoring and quickly responding to all material developments and reporting to the boards of directors as appropriate.  Processes are to be incorporated that aggregate data across multiple systems in order to develop a group wide view of liquidity risk exposures.  In addition, account operational limitations to the transferability of liquidity, funds and collateral are to be taken into account, and sufficient liquidity is to be maintained to ensure compliance during economically stressed periods with applicable legal and regulatory restrictions on the transfer of liquidity among regulated entities.  The degree of centralization in managing liquidity is to be appropriate for the depository institution’s business mix and liquidity risk profile.  In the event the Bank is subject to multiple regulatory jurisdictions, strategies and processes that recognize the potential limitations of liquidity transferability, as well as the need to meet the liquidity requirements of foreign jurisdictions, are to be in place to ensure adequate liquidity is maintained at the consolidated level and at significant legal entities; and



Regularly reporting to the Board of Directors on the liquidity risk profile of the Bank.



IF YOUR FINANCIAL INSTITUTION IS COMPRISED OF MULTIPLE ENTITIES, ALL SUCH ELEMENTS ARE TO BE FULLY SPECIFIED AND DOCUMENTED HERE FOR EACH LEGAL ENTITY AND SUBSIDIARY.



Formation of an Asset and Liability Management Committee (ALCO)



This policy falls under the direct authority of the Board of Directors, who in turn assigns authority for the formulation, revision and administration to the Asset and Liability Management Committee (ALCO).  However, the ultimate responsibility for effective asset and liability management rests with the Board of Directors.



The responsibilities conveyed to the ALCO include:



Developing an asset and liability management process and related policies and procedures;



Establishing a monitoring and reporting system;



Developing asset and liability strategies and tactics;



Submitting a written report to the Board of Directors on at least a quarterly basis; and



Ensuring on a timely basis that measurement systems adequately identify and quantify the Bank's liquidity exposure and that reporting systems communicate accurate and relevant information about the level and sources of that exposure for ALCO to fulfill its role as asset and liability manager.



Responsibilities of ALCO



In general, it is the responsibility of the ALCO to establish and monitor liquidity targets in addition to strategies and tactics to meet these targets.  Furthermore, the ALCO is to ensure that sufficient liquidity is available for unanticipated contingencies.



With respect to IRR, the ALCO is responsible for managing the structure of the Bank’s business and the level of IRR it assumes, including actively monitoring the Bank’s IRR profile.  It is responsible for ensuring that measurement systems adequately reflect the Bank’s exposure and that reporting systems adequately communicate relevant information concerning the level and sources of the Bank’s exposure.  To ensure effectiveness, the ALCO includes representatives from each major section of the Bank that assumes IRR.  Committee members are comprised of senior managers with clear lines of authority and communication over the units responsible for establishing and executing interest rate positions.  The risk management and strategic planning areas of the Bank are responsible for periodic communication to facilitate evaluations of risk arising from future business.



Other responsibilities of the ALCO include:



Holding a formal meeting on at least a quarterly basis;



Monitoring and discussing the status and results of implemented asset and liability management strategies and tactics;



Reviewing current and prospective liquidity positions and monitoring alternative funding sources;



Ensuring risk measurement systems are accurate and timely, and that relevant information regarding the level and sources of risk exposure are adequately identified and quantified;



Reviewing measurement reports and compare simulated income exposures to Bank policy limits.  Assess the expected impact of anticipated major funding shifts or changes in overall investment and lending strategy;



Reviewing the current and prospective capital levels (risk based in addition to leverage capital) to determine sufficiency thereof in relation to expected growth, interest rate risk, and asset mix and quality, regulatory requirements and dividend policy;



Reviewing outlook for interest rates and the economy at both the local and national levels;



Developing alternative strategies as deemed appropriate, which take into consideration changes in:



Interest rate levels and trends;



Deposit and loan products, and related markets;



Banking regulations; and



Monetary and fiscal policy.



Delegating day to day operating responsibilities to the Bank's Accounting Department, including specific practices and limits governing treasury operations (such as managing the Bank's discretionary portfolios, including securities, Eurocurrency, time deposits, domestic wholesale liabilities, and end user off-balance sheet transactions).





In addition, ALCO is to ensure that it is aware of the overall financial performance of the Bank and keep abreast of significant changes and trends in its financial results.  In this regard, it may:



Review actual net interest income and asset and liability distribution versus budget;



Measure performance against established standards, and if appropriate, against peer group data;



Review the current and prospective income tax position; and



Review the level and makeup of non-earning assets.



ALCO is to review the annual budget given the importance of ALCO in the management of the Bank’s balance sheet and related earnings stream.



ALCO Membership



It is the policy of the Bank that it’s ALCO must have members from each area of the Bank that directly or indirectly influence the Bank’s liquidity risk profile (e.g., lending, investment securities, wholesale and retail funding).  These committee members are to include members of Senior Management who have clear authority over the units responsible for executing liquidity related transactions and other activities within the liquidity risk management process so that ALCO directives reach these line units unimpeded.  To ensure that ALCO can control the liquidity risk arising from new products and future business activities, the committee members are to interact regularly with the Bank's risk managers and strategic planners.



To ensure the directives of this policy meet Bank specific objectives, the ALCO shall consist of the following Bank officers:



NAME, TITLE, ADDRESS, TELEPHONE AND RESPONSIBILITIES

NAME, TITLE, ADDRESS, TELEPHONE AND RESPONSIBILITIES

NAME, TITLE, ADDRESS, TELEPHONE AND RESPONSIBILITIES



The ALCO is chaired by the Bank’s CHIEF FINANCIAL OFFICER.



ALCO Meetings



The full ALCO is to meet on at least a quarterly basis.  More frequent sessions may be required in periods of increased economic activity, in times of significant unanticipated deposit flow volatility, and when specific issues require further study and follow up.  It is the responsibility of the ALCO Chairman to call unscheduled meetings.  All ALCO Committee Members are to plan their schedules around ALCO meetings.



It is the responsibility of Senior Management to ensure ALCO receives an information package that is of sufficient content so as to permit it to carry out both its stated and implied duties.  On at least a quarterly basis, a summary reporting package is to be prepared and presented to the Board of Directors by the ALCO.  The Board of Directors is to be informed of expected changes in strategies and tactics in addition to the performance and status of specific ALCO actions previously implemented.



Industry Awareness



ALCO is responsible for keeping itself, in addition to the Board of Directors, abreast of state of the art technology, procedures and industry best practices with respect to asset and liability management that are of assistance in carrying out its duties and meeting its responsibilities as managers of the Bank’s balance sheet.



Branch Management and Bank Administration



The Bank realizes that the critical first step in addressing potential liquidity problems is to understand the Bank’s operations and attempt to retain its current deposit base.  Therefore, the Bank has implemented a process in which feedback from branch management personnel is obtained to help monitor branch activity.  In addition, branch management and customer service personnel receive periodic training on how to communicate with depositors, including advising customers on how to properly title deposits and ensuring that they have an accurate understanding of their deposit insurance coverage.  In particular, depositors are made aware of the recent temporary increase in deposit insurance and the potential for full coverage of non-interest bearing transaction accounts.



It is the responsibility of branch management personnel to understand their markets and quickly identify irregular deposit trends and communicate this information to Bank Administration.  It is the responsibility of Bank Administration to:



Relay this information to Senior Management and ALCO on a regular basis;



Communicate with operations personnel regarding recommended deposit retention strategies and other such matters, including those recommended by Senior Management and ALCO;



Assist the Accounting Department in performing daily branch cash flow analysis; and



Assist the Marketing Officer in handling media inquiries and developing strategies for communicating with depositors and the public.



6.	FINANCIAL PERFORMANCE OBJECTIVES AND REPORTING



General



It is the responsibility of the ALCO and Senior Management to understand and be guided by the annual goals related to certain financial performance objectives set forth by the Board of Directors.  Among these may be targeted levels on equity (ROE), return on assets (ROA), and capital.  It should be noted that many objectives are interrelated and thus are viewed as interdependent components of the Bank’s overall financial performance.



Liquidity Goals



In general, the Bank has identified the following primary sources of funding for meeting its daily operating cash outflows, as well as seasonal and cyclical cash flow fluctuations:



DESCRIBE HERE YOUR FINANCIAL INSTITUTION’S PRIMARY FUNDING SOURCES.



The Bank realizes that liquid assets are an important source of both primary (operating liquidity) and secondary (contingent liquidity) funding.  A critical component of the Bank’s ability to effectively respond to potential liquidity stress is the availability of a cushion of highly liquid assets without legal, regulatory, or operational impediments (i.e., unencumbered) that can be sold or pledged to obtain funds in a range of stress scenarios.  These assets are to be held as insurance against a range of liquidity stress scenarios including those that involve the loss or impairment of typically available unsecured and/or secured funding sources.  The size of the cushion of such high quality liquid assets is to be supported by estimates of liquidity needs performed under the Bank’s stress testing as well as aligned with the risk tolerance and risk profile of the Bank.  Management estimates of liquidity needs during periods of stress are to be incorporated in contractual and noncontractual cash flows, including the possibility of funds being withdrawn.  In addition, such estimates are to assume the inability to obtain unsecured and uninsured funding as well as the loss or impairment of access to funds secured by assets other than the safest, most liquid assets.



It is the responsibility of ALCO and Senior Management to ensure that unencumbered, highly liquid assets are readily available and are not pledged to payment systems or clearing houses.  The quality of unencumbered liquid assets is important as it will ensure accessibility during the time of most need.  The Bank could use its holdings of high quality securities (such as U.S. Treasury securities, securities issued by U.S. government sponsored agencies, excess reserves at the central bank or similar instruments) and enter into repurchase agreements in response to the most severe stress scenarios.



It is the policy of the Bank to maintain sufficient liquid assets to cover all foreseeable demands for cash and still have excess liquid assets equal to at least PERCENT of total assets.  The liquidity ratio percentage is to be at least PERCENT, and ALCO and Senior Management must monitor and report this ratio to the Board of Directors on at least a quarterly basis.



If the Bank's liquidity ratio is less than PERCENT, lending policies shall be modified as necessary to achieve at least PERCENT liquidity as quickly as possible.



It is the responsibility of ALCO and Senior Management to monitor the Bank’s dependency on CD's to a maximum of PERCENT of total deposits.



Refer to the Contingency Funding Plan topic of the policy for detailed guidance with respect to strategies that address alternative responses to various adverse business scenarios, and plans and courses of actions for dealing with potential temporary, intermediate, and long term liquidity disruptions.



Key Objectives



The Bank will fund the substantial portion of its long term assets with core deposits.  The Bank’s dependence on net non-core funding sources is not to exceed PERCENT of long term assets.



Non-core funding sources are defined as the sum of time deposits over AMOUNT, federal funds purchased, other borrowed money, repos, and brokered deposits (post FDICIA) less than AMOUNT, minus the sum of federal funds sold, interest bearing balances, and investment securities with remaining maturities of one year or less.



Long term assets are defined as net loans, plus investment securities with remaining maturities of more than one year, plus other real estate owned.  The risk tolerance with respect to longer term assets funded by less stable funding sources is PERCENT. 



The Bank will maintain at least PERCENT of its loan portfolio in floating rate loans and fixed rate credits with maturities of one year or less.



The Bank’s objective is to maintain a rate sensitivity position that is relatively balanced as outlined in the Bank’s INTEREST RATE RISK POLICY.



As the Bank strives to maintain a loan to deposit ratio of PERCENT or greater, the Bank’s objective is to enhance liquidity of its investment portfolio as outlined in the Bank’s INVESTMENT POLICY.



In addition, a detailed explanation regarding the method used to calculate the Bank’s cost of funds is to be effectively addressed and formally documented as a matter of record.



Measuring Liquidity



The Bank has implemented the following processes for measuring and monitoring its existing liquidity position in addition to its net funding requirements.  The purpose of this liquidity analysis is to measure the Bank’s current liquidity position and its ability to meet future funding needs.  An analysis of the Bank’s current liquidity position generally involves a review of key balance sheet ratios.  An analysis of the Bank’s ability to meet future funding needs involves an analysis of projected cash inflows and outflows over various time horizons to identify potential cash imbalances.  This process includes robust methods for comprehensively projecting cash flows arising from assets, liabilities, and off-balance sheet items over an appropriate set of time horizons.  



Pro forma cash flow statements are a critical tool used by the Bank to adequately manage its liquidity risk.  Cash flow projections can range from simple spreadsheets to very detailed reports depending upon the complexity and sophistication of the Bank and its liquidity risk profile under alternative scenarios.  Given the critical importance that assumptions play in constructing measures of liquidity risk and projections of cash flows, it is the policy of the Bank to ensure that the assumptions used are reasonable, appropriate, and adequately documented.  It is the responsibility of the Board of Directors, Senior Management and ALCO to periodically review and formally approve these assumptions, with particular attention focused on the assumptions used in assessing the liquidity risk of complex assets, liabilities, and off-balance sheet positions.  Assumptions applied to positions with uncertain cash flows, including the stability of retail and brokered deposits and secondary market issuances and borrowings, are especially important when they are used to evaluate the availability of alternative sources of funds under adverse contingent liquidity scenarios.  Such scenarios include, but are not limited to, deterioration in the Bank’s asset quality or capital adequacy.



In general, it is the policy of the Bank to ensure that:



Its vulnerabilities to changing liquidity needs and liquidity capacities are appropriately assessed within meaningful time horizons (intraday, day to day, short term weekly and monthly horizons, medium term horizons of up to one year, and longer term liquidity needs of one year or more).  These assessments are to include vulnerabilities to events, activities, and strategies that can significantly strain the capability to generate internal cash; and



Assets are properly valued according to relevant financial reporting and supervisory standards.  This valuation standard takes into consideration that such valuations may deteriorate under market stress, and are taken into account when assessing the feasibility and impact of asset sales on the Bank’s liquidity position during stress events.



It is the responsibility of ALCO, Senior Management and the Chief Financial Officer to be aware of any information, such as a pending decline in earnings, an impending legal action, adverse media coverage, or a downgrade by a rating agency that could have an adverse impact on perceptions about the financial condition of the Bank.



Financial Ratio Analysis.  The measurement of liquidity is an inexact and highly subjective process.  This is largely due to the high degree of cash flow uncertainty associated with assets, liabilities, and off-balance sheet contracts.  The Bank uses a variety of financial ratios to measure its current liquidity position, including, but not limited to: 



Loans to deposits;



Liquid assets to total assets;



Volatile liabilities to total assets;



Liquid assets to volatile liabilities;



Net liquid assets to total assets;



Unpledged eligible collateral to total assets;



Net unused FHLB borrowing capacity to total assets;



Unpledged collateral to net unused FHLB borrowing capacity;



FHLB advances to FHLB Stock; and



Uninsured deposits to total deposits. 



A key issue is defining liquid assets and volatile liabilities.  Definitions vary depending on the objective or purpose of the analysis and data limitations.  The time horizon of the analysis is particularly important in defining what is and what is not liquid.  As a rule, liquid asset definitions include shorter term assets that are readily saleable and assets that mature over the near term.  In addition, liquid assets may include the sum of cash, deposits with other associations, investment securities, and mortgage pool securities.



Volatile liabilities generally include wholesale and rate sensitive deposits and short term liabilities that are likely to be withdrawn at the first hint of trouble.  These forms of “hot money” include brokered deposits, uninsured deposits, federal funds purchased, securities sold under agreements to repurchase, and other borrowings with remaining maturities of less than one year.



The following table represents the basic model used by the Bank for measuring current liquidity as it relates liquid assets to volatile liabilities.  The difference between liquid assets and volatile liabilities represents the net liquidity position (liquid assets less volatile liabilities equal net liquidity position):



Static Balance Sheet Model









🡫



Volatile



Liquid







Net Liquid

Assets









Liquid







🡩



Stable

Assets





Liabilities

And Equity



The Bank can improve its liquidity position in a number of different ways, including:



Increasing holdings of high quality liquid assets;



Shorting the maturities of assets;



Lengthening the maturities of liabilities;



Diversifying funding sources by maturity, geographic region, and by lender/depositor;



Expanding core deposits and other stable funding sources; or



Making loans that it can easily sell or securitize.



Successful liquidity management requires accurate measurement and control of the daily inflow and outflow of funds.  Advance knowledge of liquidity shortfalls makes it possible for the Bank to explore alternative ways to deal with them.  Two techniques used by the Bank for monitoring cash flows are liquidity gap analysis and liquidity forecasting as described below.



Liquidity Gap Analysis.  A liquidity gap schedule provides an analytical framework for measuring future funding needs by comparing the amount of assets and liabilities maturing over specific time intervals.  The following table presents a sample liquidity gap schedule used by the Bank:



Liquidity Gap Schedule





Less

Than 10

Days

Over 10

Days but

Less Than 3 Months

Over 3

Months but

Less 6

Months

Over 6

Months but

Less Than

One Year

1 to 5

Years

Over 5

Years and

Capital

Total



Assets

10

10

10

5

65

0

100

Liabilities &

Equity

50

30

15

0

0

5

100

Net Outflow

(assets minus liabilities

(40)

(20)

(5)

5

65

(5)

0

Cumulative Net Outflow

(40)

(60)

(65)

(60)

5

0

0



As stated in the schedule, the Bank slots assets and liabilities into different time intervals according to remaining time to maturity.  As a rule, the Bank slots assets and liabilities according to effective maturities rather than contractual maturities.  For example, the Bank treats non-maturity deposits as long term liabilities (based on estimated run off rates) rather than short term liabilities.  In this example, more liabilities than assets mature in the earlier time intervals, indicating that the Bank is borrowing short and lending long.



Negative gapping at the shorter end of the schedule (that is, borrowing short and lending long) increases the risk that the Bank will not be able to rollover maturing liabilities as they come due.  While such a position is not favorable to liquidity, it tends to enhance profitability over the long term, provided the Bank keeps the gaps within manageable bounds and the yield curve is not inverted.



One shortcoming of the liquidity gap schedule is that it does not capture projected balance sheet changes such as future loan and deposit growth.  While it is important to understand the liquidity of the Bank’s existing balance sheet, it is also important to forecast the growth of key balance sheet components, such as deposits and loans over time.



Liquidity/Cash Flow Forecasting.  Cash flow forecasting is a critical element in managing liquidity of the Bank.  The objective of cash flow forecasting is to project cash inflows and outflows over future periods.  A common practice used by the Bank is to project net funds deficits for short term (next 5-10 days), intermediate term, (3-6 months, 6-12 months), and long term (1-5 years).  By projecting cash flows for short, intermediate, and long term planning periods the Bank can significantly reduce the risk that sizable net funds deficits go unnoticed and unattended.



The following tables provide examples used by the Bank:



Short Term Liquidity Forecast





Day 1

Day 2

Day 3

Day 4

Day 5

Days 6-10

120

130

100

Net Cash Shortfall

0

25

0



*The liquid cash surplus is the level of cash and cash equivalents in excess of transactions balances required for day to day operations and any minimum reserve of cash for contingencies.



**The Bank would have to close the projected cash shortfall by increasing cash inflows (for instance by borrowing) or by reducing cash outflows on or before day 5 to avoid dipping into the reserve for contingencies.  The beginning liquid cash surplus on day 6 of zero ($0) assumes that management will address the net cash shortfall on day 5 by taking some action.  Of course, management may address that shortfall before day 5.

Liquidity Stress Test and Scenario Analysis.  Liquidity stress and scenario analysis is a critical aspect of liquidity risk management.  It is the responsibility of ALCO and Senior Management to conduct stress testing and scenario analysis in estimating the Bank’s liquidity requirements.  The magnitude and frequency of stress testing is to be commensurate with the complexity of the Bank and the level of its risk exposures.  It is especially important after long periods of good economic and financial conditions.  In addition, it is very important during periods of expansion, when new products or rapid growth occurs for which no loss data is available.  Liquidity stress and scenario analysis is to be performed on a periodic basis to:



Assess exposures;



Identify and quantify sources of potential liquidity strain and weaknesses;



Analyze possible impacts on the Bank’s cash flows, liquidity position, profitability and solvency;



Ensure the validity of liquidity planning relative to occurrences of internal turbulence at the Bank and/or to occurrences of stress, crisis, and collapse in the financial markets; and



Ensure current exposures are consistent with the Bank’s established liquidity risk tolerance.



This analysis is to be robust and relevant to the Bank’s current business model, balance sheet composition, and overall operations.  The analysis is to be capable of addressing a wide range of “what if” scenarios.



Liquidity stress testing and scenario analysis involves assessing liquidity under a variety of stresses and scenarios considering:



Short, intermediate, and long term scenarios;



Bank specific problems and issues; and



Systemic liquidity problems.



Stress testing and scenario analysis is to combine several related events and not be limited to a series of isolated instances as model based analysis tools often fail to account for event correlation, such as the following possibilities:



Price shocks for specific asset categories;



Evaporation of asset liquidity, significant losses by the Bank; and



Growth of liquidity needs because of commitments, economic conditions, recourse arrangements, off-balance sheet risks and a lack of access to funding markets.



In conducting an analysis of liquidity, ALCO and Senior Management are to consider an extreme range of possible future scenarios, such as optimistic, pessimistic, and most likely.



In estimating normal funding needs, the Bank may use historical data and account for seasonal and other effects believed to determine loan demand and deposit flows.  Alternatively, the Bank may rely on judgmental business projections, or undertake a customer by customer assessment for larger customers and apply historical relationships to the remainder.  In any respect, the following factors are to be considered:



Stressful events such as a loss of wholesale funding, a significant runoff of deposits, a sharp increase in funding costs, a sharp increase in loan demand, or a loss of access to securitization markets;



No additional borrowing capacity through the FHLB and/or the Federal Reserve Bank, in addition to collateral requirement increases on borrowing lines;



Deposit runoff due to adverse financial events or media, with special emphasis on concentrations in noninsured, commercial, high rate, and other potentially sensitive deposits;



A downgrade in the Bank’s PCA category from less than Well Capitalized (or equivalent triggering events), and the associated impacts on liquidity including brokered and high rate deposits;



Cash flow timing differences and the related assumptions among scenarios, such as in a general market crisis, the ability to sell assets may deteriorate significantly; and



The potential for unanticipated cash outflows and reduced cash inflows associated with embedded options in various assets, liabilities, and off-balance-sheet contacts.  Potential cash outflows include loan commitments, calls on loans sold with recourse and financial guarantees, payments on swap contracts and other financial derivatives, margin calls, early termination agreements, etc.



It is the responsibility of ALCO and Senior Management to discuss the results of stress tests and take remedial or mitigating actions to limit the Bank’s exposures, build up a liquidity cushion, and adjust its liquidity profile to fit its risk tolerance.  This stress testing and scenario analysis process helps management in formulating the Bank’s liquidity contingency funding plan.  As such, stress testing and contingency planning are closely intertwined.



Modeling.  The Bank utilizes the following best practices with respect to modeling:



Bases liquidity projection and stress scenarios on well substantiated assumptions and underlying principles and the model covers all the Bank’s on-/off-balance sheet activities;



Projection and stress scenarios that resemble customary methods;



Projection and stress scenarios that the Bank frequently updates, evaluates, and validates; and



Assumptions and data come from the Bank’s own empirical figures and industry peer data.



It is the responsibility of ALCO and Senior Management to review results of the liquidity projection and stress scenarios.



Spread Management



The objective of managing the rate sensitivity gap is to avoid negative spread.  Spread is managed by loan and deposit pricing, and investing.  The Bank strives to have neither the lowest nor the highest loan or deposit rates in the market.  Loans are priced in order to achieve a fair return on shareholder investment.  Deposits are priced to provide fair treatment for our customers and to be reasonably competitive without increasing the Bank's cost of funds.



The Bank's spread management goal is to price loans and deposits in order to obtain an interest spread of PERCENT.  That goal is based on the following components:



Yield on Earning Assets.  The yield on earning assets is the Bank's interest income (including loan fees) divided by its average earning assets;



Cost to Fund Earning Assets.  The cost to fund earning assets is the Bank's interest expense divided by its earning assets; and



Interest Spread.  The interest spread is the yield on earning assets less the cost to fund earning assets.



Refer to the Bank’s INTEREST RATE RISK POLICY for detailed guidance.



Net Liquid Assets, Liquidity Ratio and Pricing



The Bank utilizes a liquidity chart spreadsheet to estimate the liquid and non-liquid portions of each asset category, in addition to the volatile and reliable portions of each liability account.  To complete the process, all Bank liquid assets and all volatile liabilities are totaled.  The volatile liabilities are subtracted from the liquid assets to arrive at the Bank's liquidity position.  Zero liquidity is defined as being neither liquid nor non-liquid.  Positive liquidity numbers represent excess liquidity and negative liquidity numbers represent a non-liquid position.



By dividing the liquidity position by total average assets, the Bank arrives at a liquidity ratio.  The Bank's assets and liabilities are managed to achieve a liquidity ratio of at least +RATIO.  Since the liquidity position can be positive or negative, the ratio can be positive or negative.  A positive liquidity position means only that the Bank is more liquid than it needs to be.  A positive liquidity position is not a danger signal.  However, it may mean that the Bank is sacrificing profits unnecessarily to achieve a liquidity position that is too liquid.  A negative liquidity position represents too much non-liquidity and is cause for concern.  If the liquidity ratio is negative and larger than RATIO, the Bank is approaching a position that is dangerously non-liquid.



The yield the Bank receives on loans and investments is important only when safety and soundness requirements have been met.  With the possible execution of certain “rate sensitive” consumer markets, it is the policy of the Bank to be a SERVICE INTENSIVE financial institution which prices its deposits fairly but does not “buy” deposit business.  Large demand relationships and other financial considerations can influence CD pricing, however the Bank anticipates that it should be able to adequately fund its loan portfolio and provide adequate liquidity without engaging in above market deposit pricing.  As such, appropriate discipline in this area will have a considerable effect on the Bank’s bottom line.



It is the responsibility of ALCO and the Board of Directors to carefully and continually monitor pricing of transaction and non-transaction deposit rates and provide fair and competitive pricing while attempting to minimize interest expense.



NOTE:  The Bank realizes that it may be necessary to use “over market” pricing to fund short term borrowings on a rare occasion.  This can occur when the Bank experiences a rapid growth in loans and core deposits have not kept pace.  When this occurs, the Bank will use the shortest term of CDs possible.  Such stop gap funding may be classified as “brokered deposits” under FDICIA.  A maximum of PERCENT of the Bank’s deposits may be obtained from such “over market rate” CDs.  Repurchase agreements can also be used for this purpose.  As such, it is the responsibility of ALCO and the Board of Directors to retard loan growth should such funding be needed on an ongoing basis.



Financial Reports



The Bank maintains a variety of reports containing certain basic information used for day to day liquidity and funds management and in times of stress based upon the characteristics of the Bank and its funds management methods and practices.  Specifically, these reports provide:



Liquidity needs and the sources of funds available to meet these needs over various time horizons and scenarios, which is beneficial in determining the maturity distribution of assets and liabilities and expected funding of commitments;



List of large funds providers;



Asset yields, liability costs, net interest margins and variations both from the prior month and budget that permit an analysis of the cause of interest margin variations;



Longer term interest margin trends;



Any exceptions to policy guidelines;



Economic conditions in the Bank's trade area, interest rate projections, and any anticipated deviations from the original plan or budget;



Information concerning non-relationship or higher cost funding programs, including a listing of public funds obtained through each significant program, rates paid on each instrument,  and an average per program;



Information on maturity of the instruments, and concentrations or other limit monitoring and reporting; and



Additional types of custom reports based upon varying circumstances.



Overview of Funding Assets



Liquidity needs may be met by managing the Bank's asset structure through the sale or planned pay down of assets.  As previously stated, assets ideally have a PERCENT or more loan to deposit mix that will be maintained.  At this level, the Bank can enjoy high earnings coupled with adequate liquidity so long as credit quality and overhead are controlled.



Fed funds and other instantly available investments should provide a minimum PERCENT coverage of the Bank’s deposit base.  Periodic volatility of deposit levels may require a high number.



Other surplus funds may be placed as appropriate in other approved investments as stated in the Bank’s INVESTMENT POLICY.



The Bank’s loan portfolio is designed to be diversified as to loan category, collateral coverage, and industry concentration.  In any respect, over concentration in one or more areas is avoided by the Bank.  Loans within the Bank’s portfolio can be used as collateral for secured borrowings or sold for cash in the secondary loan market.  Sales in the secondary market provide fee income, relief from interest rate risk, and a funding source to the originating bank.



Loan commitments, such as fee paid letters of credit used as backup lines, are traditional uses of funds that are off-balance sheet.  The Bank estimates the amount of unfunded commitments that will require funding over various time horizons.  This estimate is included with anticipated demands against unfunded commitments in the Bank’s internal reporting and contingency planning process.



In any respect, the Bank realizes that assets normally assumed to be liquid sometimes are not liquidated easily and/or profitably.  For example, investment securities may be pledged against public funds and repurchase agreements, or may be depreciated heavily because of interest rate changes.  On the other hand, holding liquid assets for liquidity purposes becomes less attractive because of thin profit margins and capital maintenance requirements.  As such, the Bank strives to maintain a proper level of liquid assets through the stability of its funding structure and the potential for rapid loan portfolio expansion.  Generally, if the sources of funds are stable, established yet unused borrowing capacity is significant, and loan demand is predictable, a relatively low allowance for liquidity is required.  Factors that may indicate that a higher allowance for liquidity is required include:



The competitive environment is such that Bank customers can invest in alternative instruments;



Recent trends show substantial reduction in large liability accounts;



Substantial deposits are short term municipal special assessment type accounts;



A substantial portion of the loan portfolio consists of large problem credits with little likelihood of reduction or marketability;



A substantial portion of the loan portfolio consists of non-marketable loans (e.g., longer term, non-amortizing, non-homogeneous loans that may not be readily marketable);



The Bank expects customers to draw upon unused lines of credit or commitments in the near future;



A concentration of credits has been extended to an industry with present or anticipated financial problems;



A close relationship exists between individual demand accounts and principal employers in the trade area who have financial problems;



A significant portion of assets is pledged to support wholesale borrowings; or



Access to the capital markets is impaired.



To balance profitability and liquidity, it is the responsibility of ALCO and Senior Management to carefully weigh the full return on liquid assets (yield plus insurance value) against the expected higher return associated with less liquid assets.  Income derived from higher yielding assets may be offset if a forced sale is necessary due to adverse balance sheet fluctuations.



With respect to asset securitization, the Bank essentially transforms a pool of assets into cash.  Asset securitization typically involves the transfer or sale of on balance sheet assets to a third party who issues asset backed securities that are sold to investors in the public debt market.  Investors in these securities are paid from the cash flow received from the transferred assets.  Assets that are typically securitized include credit card receivables, automobile receivables, commercial and residential mortgage loans, commercial loans, home equity loans, and student loans.



Securitization can be an effective funding method for the Bank.  However, the Bank realizes that there are several risks associated with using securitization as a funding source such as:



Some securitizations have early amortization clauses to protect investors if the performance of the underlying assets does not meet pre-specified criteria.  If an early amortization clause is triggered, the issuing institution must begin paying principal to bondholders earlier than originally anticipated and will have to fund new receivables that would have otherwise been transferred to the trust.  The issuing institution must monitor deal performance to anticipate cash flow and funding ramifications due to early amortization clauses.



If the issuing institution has a large concentration of residual assets, the institution's overall cash flow might be dependent on the residual cash flows from the performance of the underlying assets.  If the performance of the underlying assets is worse than projected, the institution's overall cash flow will be less than anticipated.



An issuer's marketplace reputation is crucial to its ability to generate cash from future securitizations.  If this reputation is damaged, issuers might not be able to economically securitize assets and generate cash from future sales of loans to the trust.  This is especially true for institutions that are relatively new to the securitization market.  Also, if loans held for sale are funded with short term funding, the institution will have to find alternative funding sources if it is not able to sell these assets quickly.



Residual assets that the issuing institution retains are typically non-liquid assets, for which there is no active market.  Additionally, these assets are not acceptable collateral to pledge for borrowings.



In the case of the securities portfolio, the Bank may discover that securities listed as available for sale may be needed to pledge additional collateral to secure public funds or other borrowing lines.  For example, a financial institution was initially required to pledge collateral for public funds at 25 percent of the average public funds balance.  After the examination results required significant Call Report amendments, the public entity increased the collateral requirement to 125 percent of the average balance.  With no additional collateral available, the financial institution was forced to use cash as collateral to retain the deposits.  As such, a plan to sell securities as a source of liquidity depends on its effectiveness on the credit quality and marketability of these securities.



The Bank realizes that with adequate planning and certain efficiencies, securitizations can create a more liquid balance sheet, as well as leverage origination capacity.  However, peculiarities related to certain transactions, in addition to excessive reliance on securitizations as a single funding vehicle, may increase liquidity risk.  For example, a concentration or over reliance on securitizations as a funding source may increase liquidity risk if there are disruptions in the market.  Therefore, it is the responsibility of ALCO and Senior Management to carefully consider how securitizations affect the Bank’s day to day liquidity management and its contingency funding plan.  ALCO and Senior Management are to analyze the potential effect of securitizations on liquidity from an individual transaction perspective and on an aggregate basis, and make the following determinations when contemplating a securitization transaction:



The type of security (the agency securitization market remains active, but the non-agency securitization market is not);



The volume of securities scheduled to amortize during any particular period;



The plans for meeting future funding requirements (including when such requirements may arise);



The existence of early amortization or increased collateralization triggers;



The alternatives available for obtaining substantial amounts of liquidity quickly; and



The operational concerns associated with reissuing securities.



In the event the Bank uses securitizations to fund credit cards and other revolving credit receivables, ALCO and Senior Management are to prepare for the possible return of receivable balances to the balance sheet because of scheduled or early amortization.  Return of receivable balances because of scheduled or early amortization may result in large asset pools that require balance sheet funding at unexpected or inopportune times.  In addition, ALCO and Senior Management are to factor the maturity and potential funding needs of the receivables into short, intermediate, and long term liquidity planning.



Exposure may also increase if the Bank minimizes securitization costs by structuring transactions at maturities offering the lowest cost, without regard to maturity concentrations or potential long term funding requirements.  Correlating maturities of incidental securitized transactions with overall planned balance sheet growth may somewhat mitigate this risk.



Traditionally, the Bank realizes that loans are not as marketable as securities, and distressed loans are even less marketable.  Bids may be severely discounted given a financial institution’s stressed condition, and a deteriorating capital position may prevent the financial institution from realizing the sale.  Due diligence for loan sales requires time and effort.  In the event the Bank considers this option, it is the responsibility of ALCO, Senior Management and the Board of Directors to establish such business relationships by completing initial due diligence requirements in accordance with the Bank’s VENDOR MANAGEMENT PROGRAM POLICY.  Asset sales or nonrecourse loan participations may negatively affect interest income, but they can also provide short term liquidity.



Overview of Funding Liabilities



Ideally the Bank will have all stable non-interest bearing deposits, but realizes this may not always be the case.  As an alternative to using assets to satisfy liquidity needs, these needs may be met through liability sources.  Although core deposits continue to be a key liability funding source, the Bank realizes that it may experience difficulty in attracting core deposits from time to time and will look to wholesale funding sources to satisfy funding and liability management needs.  Wholesale funding sources include, but are not limited to, federal funds, public funds, FHLB or GSE advances, the Federal Reserve's primary credit program, foreign deposits, brokered deposits, and deposits obtained through the Internet or Certificate of Deposit (CD) listing services.



It is the responsibility of ALCO and Senior Management to take the following actions if engaging in wholesale borrowings:



Manage and monitor borrowing and deposit concentrations, including making a determination of whether an amount of borrowings or deposits from a single source poses an undue risk;



Review borrowing contracts and:



Determine if there are any embedded options or other features that may affect the interest rate, alter the maturity, or otherwise pose liquidity risk;



Review collateral agreements for fees, maintenance requirements, credit covenants, blanket liens, and triggers for increases in collateral; and



Monitor compliance with any credit covenants or triggers contained in the agreements.



Perform and review liquidity stress tests and:



Determine how to identify and monitor the risks of the various terms of each contract, including penalties and option features;



Perform tests before entering into any significant agreement and periodically thereafter; and



Ensure that the stress test results depict the potential impact of contractual triggers, credit covenants, and external events (such as interest rate changes that may result in the exercise of embedded options or the termination of the contract), in addition to its overall earnings and liquidity position.



Ensure that there are management processes in place to adequately measure, monitor, and control liquidity risks, including appropriate contingency funding plans;



Fully inform the Board of Directors about the risks of wholesale borrowing agreements prior to engaging in the transactions, and on an ongoing basis; and



Ensure that the instruments (especially the use of any structured instruments) are consistent with the Bank’s portfolio objectives and level of sophistication of its risk management practices (including appropriate technical knowledge and risk management systems sufficient to adequately identify, monitor, and control the risks of complex wholesale borrowings).



The use of such funding sources and the risks posed by them to the Bank vary widely depending on a variety of factors and circumstances presented.  Risks include potential increased exposure to credit, interest rate, and liquidity risk.  Wholesale funding providers are generally sensitive to changes in the credit risk profile of the Bank to which they provide these funds and to the interest rate environment.  For instance, such providers closely track the Bank's financial condition and may be likely to curtail such funding if other investment opportunities offer more attractive interest rates.  As a result, the Bank may experience liquidity problems due to lack of wholesale funding availability when needed.  Therefore, the decision whether or not to use liability sources by the Bank is based upon a complete analysis of factors such as the costs involved, concentrations, and the degree of management expertise available.
