In addition to serving as a supplement to asset liquidity, liability sources may serve as an alternative even when asset sources are available.  In any respect, the evaluation of wholesale funding by the Bank is commensurate with the degree of risk faced and used in accordance with this and other funds management policies, risk limits, strategic plans, and management expertise.



Reputation risk plays a critical role in the Bank's ability to access funds readily and at reasonable terms.  For that reason it is the responsibility of Bank personnel responsible for managing overall liquidity to be aware of any information (such as an announcement of a decline in earnings or a downgrading by a rating agency) that could affect the market's or public's perception of the soundness of the Bank.



Provided below are deposit relationships in order of preference:



Business demand and transaction time deposits tied to and significantly funding borrowing relationships of the borrower;



Transaction deposits as in above without borrowings;



CDs over AMOUNT and under AMOUNT related to transaction IPC accounts;



CDs of AMOUNT and over related to IPC transaction accounts;



CDs of any amount with no other relationships;



Repurchase agreements; and



CDs of any amount with “over market pricing.”



Clearly, the Bank is significantly better off when the Bank has diversified client relationships with transaction accounts.  Funding loans, liquidity with transitory repurchase agreements, or CDs are avoided as much as possible.  The larger proportion the Bank has of transaction accounts, the lower interest costs and the higher the margin and profitability will be for the Bank.



Bank Investment Contracts



A Bank Investment Contract (BIC) is a deposit contract between the Bank and its customer that permits the customer to deposit funds over a period of time and obligates the Bank to repay the amounts deposited plus interest at a guaranteed rate to the end of the contract term.  The contract term varies, and may range from six months to as long as ten years.  BICs are generally structured as non-transferable liabilities (i.e., not saleable in a secondary market).  Typically, customers for BICs are in most cases sponsors of employee benefit plans such as pension plans or deferred compensation plans.  BICs may provide specified periods and conditions under which additional deposits or withdrawals may be made to or from such contracts.  Therefore, the Bank's liquidity planning includes making a reasonable estimate of its cash flow from BIC funding under different interest rate scenarios.



BIC deposits are limited to PERCENT of the Bank’s total asset base.  Board of Directors approval is required to exceed these limits.



Borrowed Funds



Ensuring that dependable borrowing facilities are in place provides an additional source of liquidity to the Bank and represents an integral part of its liquidity and funds management practices.  This subtopic addresses the role and use of alternative funding sources such as FHLB advances and wholesale repurchase agreements.  Because the nature and extent of wholesale funding activities will depend greatly on the Bank’s overall balance sheet risk position, the administration of these activities primarily falls under the responsibility of ALCO.



The appropriate use of asset and liability funding sources may result in lower overall liquidity cost.  By managing borrowings in a coordinated fashion with asset liquidity needs, the Bank can tailor liabilities to fit its cash flow needs instead of apportioning asset types and amounts to a given liability base.  Locking in term funding can also reduce liquidity risk, especially if the Bank can extend the duration of its liability structure.  Accessing wholesale funds allows the Banks to obtain funds quickly and efficiently.  Nevertheless, borrowings are viewed by the Bank as a supplemental funding source, rather than as a replacement for core deposits.



Managing liquidity through adjustments to liabilities requires the Bank to plan strategies more carefully than if the Bank managed liquidity based only on assets.  As such, it is the responsibility of ALCO and Senior Management to have a complete understanding of the associated risks, commensurate risk management practices, and a comprehensive contingency funding plan that specifically addresses funding as the Bank's financial condition or the economy deteriorates when relying on borrowed funds.



The Bank considers the following key elements of risk with respect to borrowed funds:



Secured borrowings can impact the Bank's liquidity profile by pledging high quality assets, lessening the availability of such assets for contingent liquidity demands.



If the Bank's condition or the economic climate deteriorates, it will be more difficult to borrow funds economically, if at all, when needed the most.



Changes in market conditions can make it difficult for the Bank to secure funds and to manage its funding maturity structure.



Due to rate competition, the Bank may incur relatively high costs in obtaining funds and may lower credit quality standards in order to invest in higher yielding loans and securities.  If the Bank is purchasing liabilities to support assets already on its books, the high cost of borrowings may result in a negative yield spread.



Preoccupation with obtaining funds at the lowest possible cost, without proper consideration given to diversification and to maturity distribution, intensifies the Bank's exposure to funding concentrations and the risk of interest rate fluctuations, respectively.



Management might not fully understand the terms of the particular borrowings.  Some borrowings have embedded options that make maturity or future interest rate uncertain.  This uncertainty can increase the complexity of liquidity management and, under certain circumstances, may increase the cost of funding.



The extent of ALCO and Senior Management’s reaction to these risks will depend upon the mix of funding sources and the Bank’s risk tolerance (the willingness and ability of an individual or the Bank to borrow or lend money for a given risk/reward profile).  Factors affecting risk tolerance of funds providers include:



Obligations to fiduciary investors, such as money market funds, trust funds and pensions.



Reliance on rating firms.  Bylaws or internal guidelines may prohibit placing funds in banks that have low ratings.



Obligations to disclose information on investment holdings.



Self interest in maintaining an orderly marketplace.  For this reason major banks are slow in eliminating funding to other banks.



Lack of a personal contact at the bank to provide timely and accurate information about its financial condition. 



The limit established on all types of borrowings is set at PERCENT of total assets.  Board of Directors approval is required to exceed these limits.



Wholesale funding will typically be considered in the following situations:



To supplement outflows of the deposit base;



When there is a need to acquire funds quickly without impacting local market pricing;



When there is an inability to fund a specific transaction from the local market with the type/term of funds desired (such as with funds that fit the existing balance sheet structure); or



When the cost of such funds is below the marginal cost of raising local market deposits.



In addition, when deemed appropriate for purposes of managing interest rate risk and/or improving the utilization of Bank capital, wholesale funding may be used to purchase investment securities and finance activities.  However, the Board of Directors and Senior Management recognize that the terms and repricing characteristics of such borrowing and investment activities may be intentionally mismatched to achieve specific ALCO objectives.



Federal Home Loan Bank (FHLB) Advances.  The Bank is authorized to borrow when necessary from the FHLB.  FHLB advances tend to be the Bank’s primary borrowing vehicle due to both the dependability of the FHLB as a provider of funds and the availability of a broad range of maturities (overnight to 10 years or longer) with a variety of characteristics that can be tailored to the needs of the Bank.  While the FHLB lends on a collateralized basis with 1-4 family mortgages and U.S. Government and Agency securities being the collateral of choice, other loan types also qualify to varying degrees.  The Bank realizes that the FHLB may limit or deny the Bank’s request for an advance if the Bank is:



Engaging in any unsafe or unsound practice;



Inadequately capitalized;



Sustaining operating losses; and



Deficient with respect to financial or managerial resources or deficient otherwise.



Generally, the FHLB requires that collateral secure advances.  Thus, the unused borrowing capacity of the Bank is a function of both its eligible, unpledged collateral and its unused line of credit with the FHLB.  In addition, the Bank may need to increase its holdings of FHLB stock in order to access all unused borrowing capacity (FHLBs generally require members to hold stock equal to approximately five percent of outstanding advances).



Some FHLB advances contain embedded options or other features that may increase funding risk.  For example, some types of advances provide the FHLB with the option to demand repayment or increase the interest rate on the advance under specified conditions.

 

Sometimes before market information is available to other fund providers a FHLB can react quickly to reduce its exposure to a troubled institution by not rolling over unsecured lines of credit.  Depending on the severity of a troubled institution’s condition, a FHLB may take the following actions:



Apply steeper “haircuts” (the margin or difference between the actual market value of a security and the value assessed by the lending side of the transaction) to existing advances.



Apply more conservative valuations of underlying collateral.



Reduce the available line relative to total assets.



Discontinue or withdraw (at maturity) its collateralized funding program because of concerns about the quality or reliability of the collateral or other credit related concerns.



This may create significant liquidity problems for the Bank, especially if it has large amounts of short term FHLB funding.  As such, it is the policy of the Bank to aggregate FHLB funds by type of program to monitor and appropriately limit short term liability concentrations, just as with any other credit sensitive funds provider.



For FHLB borrowings (as with all borrowings to meet liquidity needs), it is the policy of the Bank to evaluate the level of its borrowings from any one source as well as the quality of the source.  It is the responsibility of ALCO and Senior Management to perform adequate due diligence in selecting funding sources, and periodically review their quality and stability.  Significant concentrations of funding from an FHLB are a risk factor and the Bank controls that risk and has viable contingency plans in place should a need arise for alternative funding sources.



Repurchase Agreements (Repo).  Securities sold under repurchase agreements are a means of financing inventories of securities.  Under repurchase agreements, securities are temporarily “loaned out,” for periods ranging from overnight to one year in return for borrowed funds.  The vast majority of repurchase agreements mature in three months or less.  A standard repurchase agreement involves the acquisition of funds through the sale of securities with a simultaneous commitment to repurchase the securities on a specified date at a specified price.  The collateral most often used by the Bank is U.S. government and agency mortgage backed securities (MBS).  The repurchase agreement rate is the interest rate that the borrower pays the lender (investor) for the use of funds.



When appropriate the Bank may utilize such borrowings collateralized by securities as an alternative source of funds.  These funds are available through the broker dealer community.  However, the Bank realizes the risks inherent in repurchase agreement transactions.  To mitigate these risks, the Bank:



Maintains established written credit policies;



Requires identification and periodic credit evaluations of each counterparty;



Establishes maximum position and exposure limits for each counterparty;



Mandates individual or master written agreements for all repurchase transactions that specify acceptable collateral types and maturities, call defaults and sellout provisions, ownership rights, substitute collateral rights, and persons authorized to transact business on behalf of both parties; and



Provide for acceptable control provisions over underlying securities.



It is the policy of the Bank regarding a plan to engage in the sale of repurchase agreements to retail customers to consult with legal counsel competent in the field of securities law to determine what constitutes sufficient disclosure to customers in addition to ensuring compliance with the antifraud, and other applicable provisions of federal and state securities law.  Repo lines may be established with Board of Directors approved brokerage firms and banks in accordance with the Bank’s VENDOR MANAGEMENT PROGRAM POLICY.



Dollar Repurchase Agreements.  Also known as dollar repos and dollar rolls, these instruments provide the Bank with an alternative method of borrowing against securities owned.  Unlike "standard" repurchase agreements, dollar repos require the buyer to return to the seller substantially similar, versus identical, securities.  Dealers typically offer dollar roll financing to the Bank as a means of covering short positions in particular securities.  Short positions arise when a dealer sells securities that it does not currently own for forward delivery.  To avoid the costs associated with failing on a delivery, dealers are willing to offer attractive financing rates in exchange for the use of the Bank's securities in covering a short position.



To qualify as borrowings, dollar repos must require the buyer to return to the seller "substantially similar" securities by the settlement date, which cannot exceed 12 months from the inception of the transaction.  Mortgage pass through securities repurchased are considered "substantially similar" to those sold if all of the following conditions are met:



Are collateralized with similar mortgages;



Are issued by the same agency and be part of the same program;



Have the same remaining weighted average maturity;



Are priced to have similar market yields;



Have identical coupon rates; and



Satisfy good delivery requirements.



In addition, securities used in dollar repo transactions must have been held in the seller's investment portfolio for a minimum of 35 consecutive days prior to the initiation of the contract.



Federal Reserve Bank Loans.  The Federal Reserve Bank can provide the Bank with short term collateralized credit via its discount window.  The Federal Reserve Bank will extend primary credit at a rate above the target Fed Funds rate on a short term basis (typically, overnight) to eligible depository institutions.  The Federal Reserve Bank bases eligibility for primary credit on an institution’s examination rating and capital status.  In general, adequately capitalized institutions with composite CAMELS ratings of 1, 2, or 3 are eligible for primary credit unless supplementary information indicates their condition is not generally sound.  Other conditions exist to determine eligibility for 4 and 5 rated institutions.



An institution eligible for primary credit need not exhaust other sources of funds before coming to the discount window.  Institutions may use primary credit to finance the sale of fed funds.  However, because of the above market price of primary credit, the Federal Reserve Board expects institutions to mainly use the discount window as a backup source of liquidity, rather than as a routine source.



Generally, the Federal Reserve Bank will extend primary credit on an overnight basis with minimal administrative requirements to eligible institutions, and also may extend primary credit to eligible institutions for periods of up to several weeks if funding is not available from other sources.  These longer extensions of credit are subject to greater administrative oversight.



In addition, the Federal Reserve Bank also offers secondary credit to institutions that do not qualify for primary credit.  Secondary credit is typically another short term backup source of liquidity.  Long term secondary credit would be available for the orderly resolution of a troubled institution.  In such a case, there are certain limitations and a higher level of Federal Reserve Bank administration and oversight.



The Bank has executed borrowing agreements and fully collateralizes all borrowings.  U.S. government securities are the most acceptable and most common type of collateral in obtaining a loan, although any "bankable" asset is acceptable for pledging.  Other acceptable collateral consists of mortgage backed, asset backed, municipal, sovereign, or corporate securities, and loans (municipal, commercial, 1-4 family residential).  Collateral may be transferred to the Federal Reserve Bank, held by the borrower in custody, held by a third party, or reflected by book entry.



Brokered and Rate Sensitive Deposits



The term "brokered deposit" means any deposit that is obtained, directly or indirectly, from or through the mediation or assistance of a deposit broker.  However, brokered deposits do not include those deposits obtained by a listing service that meets the following criteria:



The person or entity providing the listing service is compensated solely by means of subscription fees (i.e., the fees paid by subscribers as payment for their opportunity to see the rates gathered by the listing service) and/or listing fees (i.e., the fees paid by the insured depository institution as payment for the opportunity to list or "post" their rates).  The listing service does not require an insured depository institution to pay for other services offered by the listing service or its affiliates as a condition precedent to being listed.



The fees paid by an insured depository institution are flat fees, in which they are not calculated based on the number or dollar amount of deposits accepted by the insured depository institution as a result of the listing of the insured depository institution’s rates.



In exchange for fees, the listing service performs no service except the gathering and transmission of information concerning the availability of deposits.  This information may include an insured depository institution's name, address (including e-mail address), telephone number and interest rates.  Except for providing this information, the listing service does not serve as a liaison between depositors and an insured depository institution.  For example, the listing service does not pass information about a depositor to an insured depository institution.



The listing service is not involved in placing deposits or confirming the placement of deposits.  Any funds to be invested in deposit accounts are remitted directly by the depositor to the insured depository institution and not, directly or indirectly, by or through the listing service.



The term “deposit broker” means:



Any person engaged in the business of placing deposits, or facilitating the placement of deposits, of third parties with insured depository institutions, or the business of placing deposits with insured depository institutions for the purpose of selling interests in those deposits to third parties; and



An agent or trustee who establishes a deposit account to facilitate a business arrangement with an insured depository institution to use the proceeds of the account to fund a prearranged loan.



The term also includes any insured depository institution that is not well capitalized, and any employee of any such insured depository institution, which engages, directly or indirectly, in the solicitation of deposits by offering rates of interest (with respect to such deposits) which are significantly higher than the prevailing rates of interest on deposits offered by other insured depository institutions in such depository institution's normal market area.

The term does not include:



An insured depository institution, with respect to funds placed with that depository institution;



An employee of an insured depository institution, with respect to funds placed with the employing depository institution;



A trust department of an insured depository institution, if the trust or other fiduciary relationship in question has not been established for the primary purpose of placing funds with insured depository institutions;



The trustee of a pension or other employee benefit plan, with respect to funds of the plan;



A person acting as a plan administrator or an investment adviser in connection with a pension plan or other employee benefit plan provided that person is performing managerial functions with respect to the plan;



The trustee of a testamentary account;



The trustee of an irrevocable trust (other than one described above), as long as the trust in question has not been established for the primary purpose of placing funds with insured depository institutions;



A trustee or custodian of a pension or profit sharing plan qualified under section 401(d) or 403(a) of the Internal Revenue Code (26 U.S.C. 401(d) or 403(a));



An agent or nominee whose primary purpose is not the placement of funds with depository institutions; or



An insured depository institution acting as an intermediary or agent of a U.S. government department or agency for a government sponsored minority or women owned depository institution deposit program.



NOTE:  In some instances the Bank may use Internet listing services as alternatives to brokered deposits.  These deposits are not considered brokered unless the Bank is less than Well Capitalized (see below) and the rates offered exceed the guidelines established in the brokered deposit regulations.  Internet deposits can be obtained relatively quickly.  However, recent market events have revealed limitations in this funding source.  The number of Internet depositors is relatively small compared with the overall market, and the funds available from this source are limited, as each Internet depositor typically caps the amount placed at any one financial institution.  As such, if Internet deposits are part of the Bank’s liquidity plan, it is the responsibility of ALCO and Senior Management to establish agreements with listing services and periodically acquire Internet deposits to test the viability of this liquidity source.



The following represents capitalization categories with respect to brokered deposit guidelines in accordance with Section 38 of the Federal Deposit Insurance Act and Part 337.6 of the FDIC’s Rules and Regulations:



Well Capitalized.  A well capitalized insured depository institution may solicit and accept, renew or roll over any brokered deposit without restriction.



Adequately Capitalized.  An adequately capitalized insured depository institution may not accept, renew or roll over any brokered deposit unless it has applied for and been granted a waiver of this prohibition by the FDIC.  Any adequately capitalized insured depository institution that has been granted a waiver to accept, renew or roll over a brokered deposit may not pay an effective yield on any such deposit which, at the time that such deposit is accepted, renewed or rolled over, exceeds by more than 75 basis points:



The effective yield paid on deposits of comparable size and maturity in such institution's normal market area for deposits accepted from within its normal market area; or



The national rate paid on deposits of comparable size and maturity for deposits accepted outside the institution's normal market area.  For purposes of this provision, the national rate shall be a simple average of rates paid by all insured depository institutions and branches for which data are available.  This rate shall be determined by the FDIC.



Undercapitalized.  An undercapitalized insured depository institution may not accept, renew or roll over any brokered deposit.  An undercapitalized insured depository institution may not solicit deposits by offering an effective yield that exceeds by more than 75 basis points the prevailing effective yields on insured deposits of comparable maturity in such institution's normal market area or in the market area in which such deposits are being solicited.



Waiver.  The FDIC may, on a case by case basis and upon application by an adequately capitalized insured depository institution, waive the prohibition on the acceptance, renewal or rollover of brokered deposits upon a finding that such acceptance, renewal or roll over does not constitute an unsafe or unsound practice with respect to such institution.  The FDIC may conclude that it is not unsafe or unsound and may grant a waiver when the acceptance, renewal or rollover of brokered deposits is determined to pose no undue risk to the institution.  Any waiver granted may be revoked at any time by written notice to the institution.



Exclusion for Institutions in FDIC Conservatorship.  No insured depository institution for which the FDIC has been appointed conservator shall be subject to the prohibition on the acceptance, renewal or rollover of brokered deposits or Section 29 Federal Deposit Insurance Act for 90 days after the date on which the institution was placed in conservatorship.  During this 90 day period, the institution shall, nevertheless, be subject to the restriction on the payment of interest.  After such 90 day period, the institution may not accept, renew or roll over any brokered deposit.



Market.  A market is any readily defined geographical area in which the rates offered by any one insured depository institution soliciting deposits in that area may affect the rates offered by other insured depository institutions operating in the same area.  The effective yield on a deposit with an odd maturity shall be determined by interpolating between the yields offered by other insured depository institutions on deposits of the next longer and shorter maturities offered in the market.



It is presumed that the prevailing rate or effective yield in the relevant market is the national rate, unless the FDIC determines in its sole discretion based on available evidence that the effective yield in that market differs from the national rate.  Evidence of the effective yield in a particular market may include (but is not limited to) the following criteria:



Rates paid by other insured depository institutions in the same state, county or metropolitan statistical area (though the FDIC shall not be obligated to recognize each area as a separate market area);



Rates paid by credit unions in the same market area if the FDIC determines that the insured depository institution competes directly with these credit unions; and



Different rates paid on different deposit products in the same market area (though the FDIC shall not be obligated to recognize all alleged distinctions among various deposit products).  As an example for a particular market, evidence exists that the rates on money market deposit accounts (MMDAs) differ from the rates on negotiable order of withdrawal (NOW) accounts.  MMDAs are distinguishable from NOW accounts in that the two types of accounts are subject to different legal requirements.  Under these circumstances for this market, the FDIC could recognize that the prevailing rate on MMDAs is different than the prevailing rate on NOW accounts.



Typically, brokered deposit customers who focus exclusively on yield are highly rate sensitive and can be a less stable source of funding than typical relationship deposit customers.  These customers may have no other relationship with the Bank and have no loyalty with their deposit funds.  If more attractive returns become available, these customers may rapidly transfer funds to new institutions or investments in a manner similar to that of wholesale investors.  In addition, brokered deposits can quickly disappear if the market believes an institution is in trouble and might be at risk of failure.  An adverse change in perception may result in a liquidity crisis.  Should capital erode, a bank may fall below the Well Capitalized threshold under the Prompt Corrective Action rules.  Therefore, it is the responsibility of the ALCO and Senior Management to remain constantly aware of the number and magnitude of such deposits.



It is the responsibility of the Board of Directors and Senior Management to perform adequate due diligence procedures before entering into any business relationship with a deposit broker in accordance with the Bank’s VENDOR MANAGEMENT PROGRAM POLICY.  Deposit brokers are not regulated by bank regulatory agencies, and the need to properly assess potential risk to the Bank’s earnings and capital associated with brokered or other rate sensitive deposits is critical.



Brokered deposits are limited to PERCENT of the Bank’s total asset base.  Board of Directors approval is required to exceed these limits.



Deposits, Core Deposits and Cash



The critical role deposits play in the Bank's ongoing, successful operation clearly demonstrates the importance of retaining (and in most instances expanding the deposit base) and of monitoring the nature and volatility of the deposit structure.  As such, the Bank’s Deposit Management Program includes, but is not limited to, the following information:



A clearly defined marketing strategy;



Projections for deposit growth and structure;



Associated cost and interest rate scenarios;



Procedures to compare results against projections; and



Steps to revise the plan when needed.



The Bank’s Deposit Management Program takes into account the:



Makeup of the market area economy, including local and national economic conditions;



Potential for investing deposits at acceptable margins;



Management competence;



Adequacy of Bank operations;



Location and size of facilities;



Nature and degree of bank and non-bank competition; and



Effect of monetary and fiscal policies of the federal government on the Bank's service area and money and capital markets in general.



This program is monitored by ALCO and Senior Management on a periodic basis and adjusted as necessary, taking into account at minimum:



Projected deposit growth;



Makeup of the accounts as to stable deposits;



Volume and trend of total deposits;



Volume and trend of the various types of deposits offered, including fluctuating, seasonal and volatile deposits;



Maturity distribution of time deposits;



Rates being paid on each type of deposit;



Rates being paid by trade area competition;



Caps on large time deposits, public funds, and out of area deposits; and



Any other information needed or required.



In addition, this review is also to include a report that addresses funding concentration in, or excessive reliance on, any single source or type of funding, such as brokered funds, deposits obtained through the Internet or other types of advertising, and other similar rate sensitive or credit sensitive deposits.



Core deposits of the Bank are defined in the Uniform Bank Performance Report (UBPR) User Guide as the sum of demand deposits, all NOW and ATS accounts, MMDA savings, other savings deposits, and time deposits.  Core deposits are generally stable, lower cost funding sources that typically lag behind other funding sources in the need for repricing during a period of rising interest rates.  These deposits are typically funds of local customers that also have a borrowing or other relationship with the Bank.



With regards to cash on hand, the term “cash” consists of vault and teller drawer cash, reserves kept at the Federal Reserve Bank, and items in the process of collection.  Since these are non-earning assets, the Bank minimizes the balances in each category.  There is no spare cash that can be used to fill a liquidity need.



NOTE:  While cash is the essence of liquidity, the cash balances reported on the Bank’s balance sheet are not necessarily available to meet a liquidity shortfall.  While the Bank needs a minimum level of operating cash balances for day to day transactions (for tellers and ATMs), other cash balances may be in the form of checks or drafts in the process of collection, and are unavailable.  Typically, only excess cash balances (balances over and above those needed for daily operations and scheduled payments) are sources of liquidity.  However, the Bank does not generally hold large, excess cash balances that are nonearning assets.



Federal Income Tax Position



The income tax position of the Bank is managed to provide the maximum benefit to shareholders on a consistent basis.  The ALCO and the Board of Directors are responsible for reviewing the estimated tax position to determine the usefulness of tax exempt securities and other legitimate tax shelters on a quarterly basis.



International Funding Sources



As in the case of domestic sources of funds, international funding may exist in a number of ways.  The most common is the Eurodollar market.  Eurodollar deposits are dollar denominated deposits taken by the Bank's overseas branch or its international banking facility (IBF).  They can also be certificates of deposit issued by banks in Europe, with interest and principal paid in dollars.  Eurodollar certificate of deposit interest rates generally use LIBOR as the index rate.  These certificates of deposit usually have minimum denominations of $100,000 and have a short term maturity of less than two years.  They are free of reserve requirements and deposit insurance assessments.  The interbank market is highly volatile, and the Bank's Eurodollar deposit taking activity is analyzed within the same context as all other potentially volatile funding sources.



International funding sources are limited to PERCENT of the Bank’s total asset base.  Board of Directors approval is required to exceed these limits.



Large Depositors



For regulatory purposes large deposits are defined as those concentrations of funds under one control, or payable to one entity, which aggregate 2% or more of the Bank's total deposits.  By virtue of size, such deposits are considered to be potentially volatile liabilities.  However, certain large deposits can actually remain relatively stable for long periods.  Therefore, the nature of the relationship between the large depositor and the Bank is carefully evaluated when assessing the volatility of the large deposit.  For example, a member of the Board of Directors might maintain sizable deposit accounts in the Bank because of his or her relationship with the Bank.  These deposits in aggregate might be considered large deposits, but are not volatile funds due to the stability of the relationship.  Also, in reviewing large deposits the existence of related "Other Liabilities," such as borrowings and repurchase agreements, and associated loans or investment relationships is considered.



It is the policy of the Bank to carefully address within its funding strategy the potentially volatile nature of any concentration of deposits in a limited number of accounts or substantial sums maturing simultaneously.  These considerations include pledging requirements, affiliated relationships, impact on liquidity and funds management, and the normally narrow interest spreads associated with large deposits.  To the extent that fluctuations in deposit and loan volumes adversely coincide, that is, deposits are low when loans are high, special liquidity management measures must be taken.  While the comments above deal with large deposits, similar concerns exist for other concentrated sources of funding.



Large depositors are limited to PERCENT of the Bank’s total asset base.  Board of Directors approval is required to exceed these limits.



Loan Concentration Guidelines



The Bank realizes that effective loan portfolio management (LPM) is essential to the Bank’s safety and soundness.  LPM is the process by which risks that are inherent in the credit process are managed and controlled.  It is the responsibility of lending personnel to understand not only the risk posed by each credit but also how the risks of individual loans and portfolios are interrelated.  These interrelationships can multiply risk many times beyond what it would be if the risks were not related.  In general terms, the Bank uses such practices as viewing the loan portfolio in its segments and as a whole, in addition to considering the relationships among portfolio segments and among loans, to provide a more complete picture of the Bank’s credit risk profile and the use of appropriate tools to analyze and control the risk.



A primary objective of the Bank’s LPM is to control the strategic risk associated with the Bank’s lending activities.  The following elements are used to complement fundamental credit risk management principles employed by the Bank, such as sound underwriting, comprehensive financial analysis, adequate appraisal techniques and loan documentation practices, and sound internal controls:



Assessment of the credit culture;



Portfolio objectives and risk tolerance limits;



Management information systems;



Portfolio segmentation and risk diversification objectives;



Analysis of loans originated by other lenders;



Aggregate policy and underwriting exception systems;



Stress testing portfolios;



Independent and effective control functions; and



Analysis of portfolio risk/reward tradeoffs.



It is the intent of the Bank to have no one consumer or commercial product type, industry type or geographic sector with a disproportionately high level of outstandings.  A concentration of credit generally consists of direct or indirect extensions of credit and contingent obligations that, when aggregated exceed PERCENT of the Bank's capital structure (tier 1 capital plus the allowance for loan and lease losses - ALLL).  Typically, loans to related groups of borrowers, loans collateralized by a single security or securities with common characteristics and loans to borrowers with common characteristics within an industry have been included in homogeneous risk groupings when assessing asset concentrations.  A concentration may include the aggregate of all types of credit or investment in a particular homogeneous risk grouping.



THE FOLLOWING THREE PARAGRAPHS, TABLE AND NOTATION ARE APPLICABLE IF YOUR FINANCIAL INSTITUTION IS REGULATED BY THE OTS:



It is the policy of the Bank to maintain a regulatory investment limit of 20 percent of assets in commercial loans if the Bank invests at least 10 percent of assets in loans to small businesses.  The OTS does not require the Bank to aggregate commercial loans held by its service corporation with its own commercial loans in calculating compliance with the investment limit.  However, if the Bank assumes recourse, guarantees, or otherwise obligates itself in connection with the service corporation’s outside funding of its commercial lending activities, then it must include that amount in its commercial lending limit.  OTS continues to limit the Bank’s investments in service corporations to a maximum of three percent of assets.



The Bank may make loans secured by nonresidential real estate, which includes commercial real estate, in amounts up to 400 percent of capital.  Loans secured by a combination of farm residences and farmland are residential real estate and, thus, are not subject to the 400 percent limit.



The Home Owners’ Loan Act (HOLA) allows the Bank to hold loans fully guaranteed by a federal government agency without investment restrictions.  When a savings association originates or invests in loans guaranteed by the Economic Development Administration (EDA), the Small Business Administration (SBA), or the Farmers Home Administration (FmHA), only the unguaranteed portions of those loans count against the commercial lending limits.



Loans Secured by Business or Agricultural Real Estate

400% of Capital

Guaranteed Portion of EDA, SBA and FmHA Loans

No Limit

Other Commercial Loans

20% of Assets Provided at Least 10% of Assets are to Small Businesses



NOTE:  In general, the Bank’s limit of aggregate loans to one borrower is 15 percent of its unimpaired capital and unimpaired surplus.



The Bank strives to serve its community through a variety of loan programs.  The continued strong economic growth in the Bank’s defined lending area provides ample opportunity to offer several diversified lending programs.  While the Board of Directors recognizes strategies may change in upcoming years, the Bank presently desires the following parameters for its loan mix.



Loan Category	Portfolio Parameter



Commercial Loans	PERCENT - PERCENT

(Encompassing Lines of Credit / Accounts Receivable

Inventory Loans / Equipment Loans / Leasehold

Improvement Loans / Permanent SBA Loans)



Real Estate Loans	PERCENT - PERCENT

(Encompassing Home Equity Loans / Home Equity

Lines of Credit / Commercial Income Property

Loans / Multi Family Loans)



Construction Loans	PERCENT - PERCENT

(Encompassing 1-4 Family Loans / SBA 504 and 7(a)

Loans / Commercial Raw Land Development /

Residential Lot Development / Commercial Property

Construction Loans)



Consumer Loans	PERCENT - PERCENT

(Encompassing Vehicle, Boat, Aircraft & Mobile Home

Loans / Personal Lines of Credit / Visa Cards / Overdraft

Protection Loans)



In identifying asset concentrations, commercial and residential real estate loans can be viewed separately when their performance is not subject to similar economic or financial risks.  As such, commercial real estate development loans need not be grouped with residential development loans, especially when the residential developer has firm, reliable purchase contracts for the sale of the homes upon their completion.



In general, the Bank maintains effective internal policies, procedures, processes, systems and controls to identify, measure, monitor, and control its credit risk concentrations.  In addition, the Bank routinely assesses the extent of the Bank’s credit risk concentrations in its assessment of capital adequacy to cover the different forms of credit risk concentrations to which the Bank is exposed, such as:



Significant exposure to an individual counterparty or group of related counterparties;



Credit exposures to counterparties in the same economic sector or geographic region;



Credit exposures to counterparties whose financial performance is dependent on the same activity or commodity; and



Indirect credit exposures arising from the Bank’s commercial real estate (CRE) activities (e.g., exposure to a single collateral type or to credit protection provided by a single counterparty).



SPECIFICALLY DEFINE HERE HOW YOUR FINANCIAL INSTITUTION CALCULATES ITS CONCENTRATIONS AND CORRESPONDING LIMITS, INCLUDING LIMITS IN RELATION TO THE BANK’S CAPITAL, TOTAL ASSETS, AND ITS OVERALL RISK LEVEL.



Accordingly, on at least a FREQUENCY basis, it is the responsibility of Credit Administration and the Chief Credit Officer to provide a report to Senior Management and the Board of Directors identifying the distribution of the Bank's loan portfolio.  The report is to include existing concentration levels and identification of the risk that each category presents in the current economic climate.  In addition, the report is to summarize the results of stress tests of the Bank’s major credit risk concentrations to identify and respond to potential changes in market conditions that could adversely impact the Bank’s performance.  It is the responsibility of Senior Management, with concurrence and approval from the Board of Directors, to maintain appropriate limits to the level of concentration exposure in any particular grouping or category deemed prudent based on market conditions and the capital structure of the Bank.  In this respect, Credit Administration and the Chief Credit Officer are to monitor market conditions and make recommendations to Senior Management to increase or decrease exposure in various categories as warranted.



Refer to the Bank’s CONSUMER LOAN POLICY, COMMERCIAL LOAN POLICY and REAL ESTATE LOAN POLICY for detailed guidance with respect to the Bank’s lending operations in those areas.



Loan Demand and Pricing



The Bank can depend on a certain amount of loan repayment each month and on a continuing demand for new loans.  In the mortgage loan area, the Bank may be able to stop generating new loans without causing undue damage.  However, in most other areas the Bank could not realistically stop the lending process.  The approval processes could be tightened, but it is not the intent of the Board of Directors to refuse good loans to established customers.



Loan demand tends to be cyclical in nature and total loans tend to peak at the very time it is most non-liquid.  The Bank's method of estimating future loan demand is similar to the way it estimates deposit runoff.  To estimate future loan demand, historical loan information is plotted on graphs and extended into the future.  To cover contingency situations, PERCENT is added to the total.  This results in loans being negative liquid assets.



Refer to the Bank’s LOAN LOSS RESERVE (ALLL) POLICY for detailed guidance.



The following outlines the Bank’s basic loan pricing methodologies:



Floating Rates and Fixed Rates with Maturities of Less Than One Year.  As the majority, normally at least PERCENT of the Bank’s loan portfolio will be rate sensitive.  Rates will be set at a fixed amount over the Bank’s Base Rate (which normally would equal the “Wall Street Journal” Prime Rate).  Most floating rate loans should have a “floor” to protect against a great fall in rates.  Many loans will have a ceiling as well, and the level of the ceiling will vary with maturity (the longer the term, the higher the ceiling).  The actual pricing will be set by the approving authority (Senior Management and the Board of Directors’ Loan Committee).  Lower floors may be necessary in times of lower rates, but such lower floors may be allowed when necessary to maintain a competitive position in the loan market.



Historically and as a matter of policy, the Bank has normally charged a minimum of Base Rate plus PERCENT.  Rates below this level should be an exception.



Fixed Rates.  Even with the rate fluctuations over recent years, some fixed rate lending is prudent, and in fact can, under certain circumstances, greatly enhance profitability.  With regards to fixed rate lending:



Amounts extended and maturity dates should not be considered as to periods of time.  The Bank should straddle the market as to rates and maturities.  TIME PERIOD is normally the longest maturity considered with TIME PERIOD the maximum and only for exceptional high quality customers.  Occasionally, longer terms may be necessary to sell repossessed real estate.



Fixed rate loans should be priced at premium over what a comparable floating rate loan would be.  For example, if the base rate is 10% and a base plus 2% is appropriate, then a fixed rate higher that 12% should be set with the rate going higher with longer maturities.



Prepayment penalties and larger loan fees may also be appropriate with fixed rate term loans.



Consideration of whether rates are in a “rising” or falling” trend may be important in addition to whether rates are relatively “high” or “low.”  However, the volume of fixed rate lending is to be limited and the Bank’s approach to fixed rate lending should remain NON SPECULATIVE.



Negotiable and Brokered Certificates of Deposit



Negotiable certificates of deposit (CDs) warrant special attention by the Bank as a component of large deposits.  They are usually issued in denominations of AMOUNT or more and may be issued at face value with a stated rate of interest or at a discount similar to U.S. Treasury Bills.  These types of CDs may be widely traded, offer substantial liquidity, and are the underlying instruments for a market in financial futures.  They are instruments ordinarily used to fund reinvestment goals of issuing banks as opposed to solving liquidity crises.  Their cost and availability are closely related to overall market conditions.  Any adverse publicity involving either a particular bank or banks in general can impact the CD market.  These CDs have many features of borrowings and can be quite volatile.  Fundamentally, there is little to distinguish these accounts from borrowings, but negotiable CDs are clearly a form of purchased funds.  Intense competition for funds among financial intermediaries has led to the common use of CDs.  Thus, as a practical matter, drawing technical distinctions between CDs (except for the purposes of deposit insurance or deposit assessments) as borrowings or deposits is, in large measure, academic.



These types of CDs are limited to PERCENT of the Bank’s total asset base.  Board of Directors approval is required to exceed these limits.



When appropriate the Bank may utilize brokered certificates of deposit (CDs) as an alternative source of funds for reasons outlined above under the Borrowed Funds subtopic.  Since brokered CDs do not require collateral, they have the potential for being less reliable than other forms of wholesale funding and must be managed accordingly.



Brokered deposits are limited to PERCENT of the Bank’s total asset base.  Board of Directors approval is required to exceed these limits.



Public Funds



Public funds are deposit accounts of public bodies, such as state or local municipalities.  These types of deposits are typically secured and fluctuate on a seasonal basis due to timing differences between tax collections and expenditures.  General economic conditions can also be a factor in assessing the volatility of such deposits, since public entities may experience revenue shortfalls in times of economic decline.  Though regarded as generally volatile, these accounts can be reasonably stable over time, or their fluctuations quite predictable.  Local municipal deposits, for example, are often required to be maintained in the local community and, therefore, may display greater stability.  However, state and certain local deposits can be bid type deposits that may tend to be less stable.



Public funds are limited to PERCENT of the Bank’s total asset base.  Board of Directors approval is required to exceed these limits.



Purchased Funds



Purchased funds are non-core deposits and include the following:



All certificates of deposits with a face value of AMOUNT or more;



Federal funds purchased;



Repurchase agreements;



Federal Reserve advances (discount window);



Commercial paper purchased; and



All other short term borrowings.  The following represents individual and aggregate limits on borrowed funds by type and source, or a minimum limit on the amount of short term investments:



DESCRIBE HERE IN DETAIL.



For the purposes of liquidity management, federal funds purchased are deemed totally volatile as this is partly due to the overnight nature of these funds.  It is also due to reluctance on the part of the Board of Directors to become dependent on the purchase of federal funds.  In addition, the quality of investment and the depository institution should be predominant in the Bank’s decision as to funds placement, not the yield received.



It is recognized that the levels and composition of the Bank's purchased funds structure heavily impacts the success of its rate sensitivity and liquidity management decisions.  As such, purchased funds are to be kept below PERCENT of total deposits.



Secured and Preferred Deposits



Secured and preferred deposits impose pledging requirements upon the Bank.  The Bank must secure U.S. government deposits, and most states authorize or require the pledge of assets to secure state and municipal deposits.  Although several forms of security may be acceptable, U.S. government securities are the most commonly pledged.  Many states also mandate that depositories secure trust department funds deposited in their own bank, and bankruptcy court funds are often accorded similar treatment.  In addition to strict regulatory or bookkeeping controls associated with pledging requirements, the Bank has established various monitoring controls due to the impact pledging may have on liquidity.



Secured and preferred deposits are limited to PERCENT of the Bank’s total asset base.  Board of Directors approval is required to exceed these limits.



Treasury, Tax and Loan Accounts



With respect to Treasury Tax and Loan (TT&L) funds, the Bank has the option of remitting those funds daily through a Federal Reserve Bank (remittance option) or maintaining those funds in an interest bearing, demand account (note option).  The note option permits the Bank to retain the TT&L funds as secured, purchased funds callable on demand.  Under the note option, such funds are shown as deposits on the day received and as "Other Borrowed Money" on the following day.  As borrowed funds, they must be analyzed by the Bank as any other volatile funding source, which requires the encumbrance of assets for pledging purposes.



7.	CONTINGENCY FUNDING PLAN



General



It is the policy of the Bank to implement and maintain an effective Contingency Funding Plan (CFP) that identifies maximum and minimum liquidity needs and weighs alternative courses of action designed to address these needs in emergency situations.  The Bank realizes that funding decisions can be influenced by unplanned events and unanticipated stressful scenarios that can result in a significant erosion of Bank specific or general market liquidity.  Such events include, but are not limited to, the:



Inability to fund asset growth;



Difficulty renewing or replacing maturing funding liabilities;



Customers unexpectedly exercising options to withdraw deposits, draw down lines of credit, or exercise off-balance sheet commitments;



Changes in market value and price volatility of various asset types;



Changes in economic conditions, market perception, or dislocations in the financial markets;



Disturbances in payment and settlement systems due to operational or local disasters;



Legal or operational risks; and



Demise of a business line.



The Bank’s CFP is part of its comprehensive liquidity risk management program that is designed to address alternative funding if initial projections of funding sources and uses are incorrect or if a liquidity crisis arises.  This strategy can be adopted quickly, and is a cash flow projection and comprehensive funding plan that forecasts funding needs and funding sources under market scenarios including aggressive asset growth or rapid liability erosion.  The plan represents the Bank’s best estimate of balance sheet changes that may result from a liquidity or credit event, or other adverse scenarios to prudently and effectively manage routine and extraordinary fluctuations in liquidity.  The plan also helps management monitor liquidity risk, ensure that an appropriate amount of liquid assets is maintained, measure and project funding requirements during various scenarios, and manage access to funding sources.  Potential scenarios that may affect the Bank’s liquidity position include a decline in revenue due to such factors as a prolonged period in which investment prices fall, the loss of a key officer, or the loss of a significant account relationship.  Litigation, fraud, and other potential impairments can also have a material impact on the Bank’s liquidity needs.  In addition, the plan can help control day to day liquidity risk by showing that the Bank, even if it is in financial trouble, can find sources of funds to cover its funding needs.



The Bank reviews its funding position if the CFP predicts more uses of funds than sources in a near term scenario.  To reduce funding and liquidity risks, the Bank generally either:



Replaces credit sensitive liabilities with more stable, credit insensitive funding such as long term borrowing or retail deposits; or



Reduces assets that require term funding, such as loans.



This plan helps ensure the Bank can prudently and efficiently manage routine and extraordinary fluctuations in liquidity.



NOTE:  It is the responsibility of the Board of Directors, Senior Management and ALCO to be prepared for the specific contingencies that will be applicable if the Bank becomes less than “Well Capitalized” pursuant to Prompt Correction Action (PCA) provisions under the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation Improvement Act.  Contingencies may include restricted rates paid for deposits, the need to seek approval from the FDIC/NCUA to accept brokered deposits, and the inability to accept any brokered deposits.  Section 38 of the FDI Act requires insured depository institutions that are not well capitalized to receive approval prior to engaging in certain activities.  Section 38 restricts or prohibits certain activities and requires an insured depository institution to submit a capital restoration plan when it becomes undercapitalized.  Section 216 of the Federal Credit Union Act and part 702 of the NCUA Rules and Regulations establish the requirements and restrictions for federally insured credit unions under Prompt Corrective Action.  For brokered, nonmember deposits, additional restrictions apply to federal credit unions as given in parts 701.32 and 742 of the NCUA Rules and Regulations.



Plan Overview and Considerations



The Bank’s CFP is designed to be commensurate with the Bank’s complexity, liquidity and overall risk exposure profile, activities, products, organizational structure, and identifies the types of stress events which may be faced.  In general, the plan anticipates all of the Bank's funding and liquidity needs by incorporating the following key elements:



Assignment of Responsibility.  The Bank has defined responsibilities, assigning decision making authority and escalation procedures so that all personnel understand their role during a problem funding situation.  This includes evaluating the need to obtain additional liquidity expertise (internally and/or via a third party) to effectively implement the plan.  As such, the Bank has assembled a Liquidity Crisis Management Team and administrative structure that is responsible for executing realistic action plans for given levels of stress.  The team is comprised of the following members:



NAME, TITLE, ADDRESS, TELEPHONE AND RESPONSIBILITIES

NAME, TITLE, ADDRESS, TELEPHONE AND RESPONSIBILITIES

NAME, TITLE, ADDRESS, TELEPHONE AND RESPONSIBILITIES



Frequent communication and reporting among team members, the Board of Directors, and other affected managers will optimize the effectiveness of the CFP during an adverse liquidity event by ensuring that business decisions are coordinated to minimize further disruptions to liquidity.  Such events may also require the daily computation of regular liquidity risk reports and supplemental information, whereas more frequent and detailed reporting may be required as the stress situation intensifies. 



Stress Event Identification and Assessment.  It is the responsibility of the Liquidity Crisis Management Team to periodically perform an assessment of the possible liquidity events that the Bank might encounter.  Generally, the types of potential liquidity events considered range from high probability to low impact events that can occur in day to day operations, to low probability or high impact events that can arise through institution specific, systemic market, or operational circumstances.  Possible stress events may include, but are not limited to:



Inadequate capital or decline in earnings;



Deposit concentrations (hot money);



A change in credit rating by a rating agency;



Increase in nonperforming assets and/or an overall deterioration in asset quality;



Excessive interest rate risk;



Becoming less than well capitalized;



Inadequate cash flow planning;



Funding unplanned asset growth;



Expanding business horizon;



Acquisitions;



The loss of access to market funding sources;



Operational problems such as fraud and recognizing operating losses;



Weak or deficient management;



Suffering negative press coverage;



Tax implications;



Failure of one or more large financial organizations involved in banking, securities, and/or insurance;



Crisis in or collapse of the domestic or foreign financial markets;



Shortages of commodities, goods, and services, such as energy or food;



Widespread failure of information or communication systems and capabilities including the Internet;



Catastrophic events such as those arising from war, terrorism, weather, or a pandemic situation; and



Other events that may call into question the Bank's ability to meet its obligations.



The overall impact of a given stress event is to be considered, including both direct and indirect effects.



Funding and investment strategies that are concentrated in one business line or relationship are typically at greater risk of being disrupted by adverse events.  As such, the Bank periodically updates the CFP and examines contracts and arrangements associated with major lines of business and funding sources to identify low probability and/or high impact events that could adversely affect liquidity using the following matrix:





Event Probability

Impact Potential

Low

Impact Potential

High

LIST EVENT HERE

High

High Probability

Low Impact

High Probability

High Impact



Low

Low Probability

Low Impact

Low Probability

High Impact



The Bank periodically assesses responses to the events identified in the context of the Bank’s short, intermediate, and long term liquidity position.  In addition, the Bank ensures that an adequate diversification of potential funding sources focuses on:



The number of fund providers;



The underlying stability, availability, and flexibility of the sources of funds; and



The liquidity event addressed. 



In this respect, the Bank minimizes the exposure to such events and maintains plans that incorporate practical solutions that can be adopted quickly to address such contingencies should they arise. 



Assessing Levels of Severity and Timing.  It is the responsibility of the Liquidity Crisis Management Team to properly delineate the various levels of stress severity that can occur during a contingent liquidity event and identify the different stages for each type of event.  The events, stages, and severity levels identified are to include temporary disruptions, in addition to those that might be more intermediate or longer term.  The Bank uses the different stages or levels of identified severity as early warning indicators, to assess potential funding needs at various points in a developing crisis, and to specify comprehensive action plans.  The length of the scenario is to be determined by the type of stress event being modeled and encompass the duration of the event by management.



Assessing Funding Sources and Needs.  It is the responsibility of the Liquidity Crisis Management Team to perform quantitative projections and evaluations of expected funding needs and funding capacity during a liquidity stress event.  This entails an analysis of the potential erosion (magnitude and rate of outflow) in funding at alternative stages or severity levels under optimistic, pessimistic, and status quo scenarios of the stress event and the potential cash flow mismatches that may occur during the various stress levels.  The analysis is to be based on realistic assessments of the behavior of funds providers during the event and incorporate alternative contingency funding sources, and the analysis is to include all material on and off-balance sheet cash flows and their related effects.  The result should be a realistic analysis of cash inflows, outflows, and funds availability at different time intervals during the potential liquidity stress event in order to measure the institution’s ability to fund operations.  Common tools to assess funding mismatches include:



Liquidity Gap Analysis.  A cash flow report that essentially represents a best case estimate of where funding surpluses and shortfalls will occur over various future time frames.



Stress Tests.  A pro forma cash flow report with the ability to estimate future funding surpluses and shortfalls under various liquidity stress scenarios and the Bank’s ability to fund expected asset growth projections or sustain an orderly liquidation of assets under various stress events.



Identifying and Assessing the Adequacy of Contingent Funding Sources.  Because liquidity pressures may spread from one funding source to another during a significant liquidity event, it is the responsibility of the Liquidity Crisis Management Team to identify alternative sources of liquidity and ensure ready access to contingent funding sources, such as:



Any backup facilities (lines of credit);



The conditions related to their use and the circumstances when the Bank might use them, and understanding the various legal, financial and logistical constraints (such as notice periods, collateral requirements, or net worth covenants) that could affect the Bank’s ability to use backup lines and test the Bank’s ability to borrow from established backup line facilities through actual borrowing to ensure that they are valid and that no impediments or complications arise.



In some cases, these funding sources may rarely be used in the normal course of business.  Therefore, advance planning and periodic testing is to be conducted to ensure that contingent funding sources are readily available when needed.



Monitoring Potential Liquidity Stress Events.  It is the responsibility of the Liquidity Crisis Management Team to monitor for potential liquidity stress events by using early warning indicators and event triggers which are tailored to meet the Bank’s specific liquidity risk profile.  The early recognition of potential events allows the Bank to position itself into progressive states of readiness as the event evolves, while providing a framework to report or communicate within the Bank and to outside parties.  Early warning signals may include, but are not limited to, negative publicity concerning an asset class owned by the Bank, increased potential for deterioration in the Bank’s financial condition, widening debt or credit default swap spreads, and increased concerns over the funding of off-balance sheet items.



To mitigate the potential for reputation contagion, effective communication with counterparties, credit rating agencies, and other stakeholders when liquidity problems arise is of vital importance.  It is the responsibility of the Liquidity Crisis Management Team, in coordination with the Bank’s Marketing Officer and agency, to effectively interact with the media (i.e., press releases, inquiries, etc.) during a liquidity event.  In addition, group wide contingency funding plans, liquidity cushions, and multiple sources of funding are mechanisms that may mitigate reputation concerns.



Other Considerations.  It is the responsibility of the Liquidity Crisis Management Team to:



Assess the potential for triggering restrictions on the Bank’s access to brokered and high cost deposits under PCA standards and the effect on the Bank’s liability structure;



Assess the potential liquidity risk posed by other activities such as asset sales and securitization programs due to the unexpected funding needs associated with an early amortization event or disruption of warehouse funding;



Recognize the potentially higher margin or collateral requirements that may be triggered upon the deterioration of a specific portfolio of exposures or the overall financial condition of the Bank in the event the Bank relies upon secured funding sources.  Potential collateral values are to be subject to stress tests since devaluations or market uncertainty could reduce the amount of contingent funding that can be obtained from pledging a given asset.  Additionally, triggering events are to be understood and monitored;



Match potential sources and uses of funds;



Identify the sequence in which sources of funds will be used for contingent needs, although the uncertainty of the magnitude and timing of available resources may call for different priorities in different situations;



Address implementation issues such as procedures the Bank is to use to obtain emergency funds or release funds from one use to transfer to another.  This step should ensure that there are no constraints, such as blanket liens on all collateral, which may limit availability of other liquidity sources;



Address procedures to ensure funds will meet the overnight cash letter;



Include an asset tracking system that monitors which assets are immediately available for pledging or sale and how much a cash sale of these assets will generate;



Review contracts for provisions that may allow funds providers to limit or cancel access to liquidity lines;



Continually monitor wholesale funding sources to understand any changes in guidelines or collateral requirements; and



Identify other actions necessary in the event of an unexpected contingency.



Plan Testing.  It is the responsibility of the Liquidity Crisis Management Team to regularly test and update various elements of the CFP to assess the plan’s reliability under times of stress and to ensure that the plan is operationally sound.  For certain components of the plan, affirmative testing (e.g., liquidation of assets) may be impractical.  In these instances, the Liquidity Management Team is to test operational components of the CFP such as ensuring:



Roles and responsibilities are up to date and appropriate;



Legal and operational documents are up to date and appropriate,



Cash and collateral can be moved where and when needed; and



Contingent liquidity lines can be drawn when needed.



In the event the Bank rarely uses the type of funds it has identified as standby sources of liquidity in a stress situation (such as the sale or securitization of loans, securities repurchase agreements, Federal Reserve discount window borrowing, or other sources of funds), the Liquidity Crisis Management Team is to periodically test the operational elements of these sources to ensure that they work as anticipated.  



Operational simulations are to be utilized to test communications, coordination, and decision making involving managers with different responsibilities, in different geographic locations, or at different operating subsidiaries.  Simulations or tests run late in the day can highlight specific problems such as difficulty in selling assets or borrowing new funds at a time when business in the capital markets may be less active.



NOTE:  It should be realized that during real stress events, prior market access testing does not guarantee that these funding sources will remain available within the same time frames and/or on the same terms.



The Bank realizes that it may find that traditional and primary sources of liquidity are unavailable to ease liquidity problems regardless of whether the liquidity problems are institution specific or systemic.  For example, the FHLB and the Federal Reserve System might restrict the availability or terms of additional borrowings and brokered deposits may no longer be available or even permissible.  Borrowing lines with private lenders may be restricted or unavailable.



It is important to note that in the event the Bank relies on securitization or other originates to distribute business models, particular attention is to be directed to scenarios when securitization or asset sales become rapidly unavailable.  In this respect, the Bank maintains plans to address:



Disruptions in the capital markets that would result in delayed sales of receivables and/or loans;



Increased volume of putbacks, which is monitored commensurate with the nature of assets sold or securitized;



Required increases in retained interests and other credit enhancements; and



Early amortization events.



To evaluate the Bank's funding needs and strategies under changing market circumstances, the plan projects the Bank's funding position during both temporary and long term liquidity changes, including those caused by liability erosion (primarily due to sensitivity to credit risk of funds providers).  Liquidity scenarios used by the Bank include:



A temporary disruption in liquidity when funding is required only for a short time because the problem is self correcting and circumstances are expected to return to normal quickly.  Examples would be a significant operational breakdown, wire transfer outage, or physical emergency.



Longer term distressed environments, such as those used in bank rating agency definitions, which are often the risk standards used by wholesale funds providers.  Any other processes to define scenarios can be used, depending on Senior Management’s preference and the Bank’s characteristics.



At least one scenario in which the Bank is no longer considered to be investment grade.  In this scenario, appropriate actions would be to generally minimize growth and to identify, prioritize, and sell assets to reduce funding needs.



The plan also identifies, quantifies, and ranks all sources of funding by preference, including:



Reducing assets;



Modifying the liability structure or increasing liabilities;



Using off-balance sheet sources, such as securitizations; and



Using other alternatives for controlling balance sheet changes.



In addition, the plan also considers asset side strategies for responding to a liquidity crisis, including:



Whether to liquidate surplus money market assets;



When (if at all) HTM securities might be liquidated;



Whether to sell liquid securities in the repo markets; and



When to sell longer term assets, fixed assets, or certain lines of business.



The Bank’s general liability funding strategies are as follows:



Coordinating lead bank funding with that of the company's other banks and nonbank affiliates.



Establishing an overall pricing policy for funding that sets a maximum premium (or prohibiting any premium to avoid all appearance of increased risk).



Identifying dealers who will assist in maintaining orderly markets in the Bank's negotiable instruments.



Identifying particular funding markets to avoid, such as high visibility accounts (in favor of individual private contracts).



Developing strategies on how to interact with nontraditional funding sources (e.g., whom to contact, what type of information and how much detail should be provided, who will be available for further questions, and how to ensure that communications are consistent).



Setting forth a policy for early redemption requests by retail customers that is consistent with retail account disclosures and applied consistently to avoid the appearance of favoritism or discrimination.  In addition, a policy regarding wholesale customers who request early payout of their contracts is also provided for.



Estimating the Bank's potential Federal Reserve Bank discount window borrowings, if any, stipulating timing, duration, and source of repayment.



Restrictions on Use of Brokered and High Rate Deposits



It is the policy of the Bank to account for the limitations set forth in Section 38 of the Federal Deposit Insurance Act and Part 337.6 of the FDIC’s Rules and Regulations for both brokered and high rate deposits, specifically that these limits can result in deposit run off.  In the event the Bank is classified as adequately capitalized, it must obtain a waiver from the FDIC in order to use a deposit broker.  In the event the Bank is classified as less than adequately capitalized, it is precluded from using a deposit broker and offering deposit interest rates that are significantly higher than the prevailing rates in the Bank's normal market area or the national rate, as may be appropriate.



It is the responsibility of the Chief Financial Officer, ALCO and Senior Management to be familiar with all aspects of brokered deposit restrictions and limitations.  In the event the Bank uses a deposit broker or pays above normal interest rates to attract deposits, or could plausibly become less than well capitalized, it is important for management to be aware of whether the brokered deposit restrictions and interest rate limits could constrain the Bank’s ability to attract deposits and plan accordingly.



In general, the FDIC may (on a case by case basis and upon application by an adequately capitalized insured depository institution) waive the prohibition on the acceptance, renewal, or rollover of brokered deposits upon a finding that such acceptance, renewal, or roll over does not constitute an unsafe or unsound practice with respect to such institution.  The FDIC may conclude that it is not unsafe or unsound and may grant a waiver when the acceptance, renewal, or rollover of brokered deposits is determined to pose no undue risk to the institution.  Any waiver granted may be revoked at any time by written notice to the institution.



The Bank realizes that it does not assume that the FDIC will grant a waiver in the event the Bank becomes adequately capitalized and has established the following plans under such a scenario to replace or offset the need for such funding as it comes due.  In many cases, approval of a waiver request is conditioned on an institution's credible plan to limit growth, reduce its risk exposure, and return to a well capitalized position.  In addition, an undercapitalized insured depository institution may not accept, renew, or roll over any brokered deposit and is not eligible for a waiver.



LIST HERE SPECIFIC PLANS.



Even if an adequately capitalized institution obtains a waiver, interest rate restrictions apply.  In addition to brokered deposit restrictions, limits exist for deposit rates that can be paid by institutions that are less than well capitalized.  In general, any insured depository institution that is less than well capitalized may not pay an effective yield on any deposit that exceeds 75 basis points of either of the following rates, depending on its circumstances:



The effective yield paid on comparable deposits within its normal market area; or



120% of the current yield on similar maturity U.S. Treasury obligations.  In the case of any deposit at least half of which is uninsured, 130% of such applicable yield.



Additional rate restrictions apply for undercapitalized institutions.



LIST HERE YOUR FINANCIAL INSTITUTION’S MARKET AREA AND HOW EFFECTIVE YIELDS IN THE AREA WILL BE CALCULATED.



Refer to the Brokered and Rate Sensitive Deposits subtopic of the Financial Performance Objectives and Reporting topic of this policy for detailed regulatory guidance.



Basic Liquidity Crisis Action Guidelines



In the event of a liquidity crisis, it is the responsibility of Liquidity Crisis Management Team to:



Assign the Chief Financial Officer as the individual responsible for initiating external contacts with regulators, analysts, investors, external auditors, press, significant customers, and others.
